days.title: Personalpronomen und das Verb sein
days.objective: Say who you and other people are using all six personal pronouns with the correct present-tense form of sein.
days.paretoFocus: sein is the single most frequent verb in German and it carries almost every introduction, description and identification you will ever make.
drills: Say the full paradigm of sein out loud three times, pointing at yourself or an imagined person for each pronoun.
Describe five people you know with one sentence each, using a different pronoun every time.
Cover the verb forms on a written list and rebuild each one from the pronoun alone.

items: Which form of sein goes with ihr, and which goes with wir?
How do you say 'She is a teacher' without using any article?
What is the difference between sie sind and Sie sind when you hear them spoken?

activities.title: Ich bin, du bist, er ist
activities.intro: German has six personal pronouns that matter in the present tense: ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr and sie. The verb sein is completely irregular, so its forms have to be memorised as a block rather than built from a stem. Learn the pronoun and the form together as one sound.

items.en: I am a student.
items.note: German drops the article before a profession or a role after sein.

items.en: You are very nice.
items.note: du is the informal you for one person.

items.en: She is a teacher.
items.note: er ist, sie ist and es ist all share the same form.

items.en: We are from Germany.
items.note: sie sind and Sie sind use this same form.

items.en: You are on time.
items.note: ihr is the informal you for a group, and seid is spelled without a t at the end.

activities.title: Formen von sein
activities.intro: Practise matching each personal pronoun to the right form of sein.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Wir ___ heute in Hamburg.
questions.explanation: wir always takes sind. The forms bin, bist and seid belong to ich, du and ihr.

questions.prompt: Which line pairs the pronoun ihr with the right verb form?
questions.explanation: ihr takes seid. sind belongs to wir and sie, bist to du, bin to ich.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right form of sein: Du ___ sehr freundlich.
questions.explanation: The pronoun du always takes the form bist in the present tense.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right form of sein: Anna ___ Ärztin.
questions.explanation: A single name works like sie, so the verb form is ist. Note that the profession stands with no article.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am from Germany.'
questions.explanation: The subject comes first and the conjugated verb bin follows it immediately in second position.

activities.title: Wer bist du?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You meet someone at a language course and you both introduce yourselves using only forms of sein.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hallo! Ich bin Lukas. Wer bist du?
lines.en: Hello! I am Lukas. Who are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hallo Lukas! Ich bin Sofia.
lines.en: Hello Lukas! I am Sofia.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bist du aus Deutschland?
lines.en: Are you from Germany?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich bin aus Spanien.
lines.en: No, I am from Spain.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bist du Studentin?
lines.en: Are you a student?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich bin Studentin. Und du?
lines.en: Yes, I am a student. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich bin Lehrer. Wir sind hier zusammen.
lines.en: I am a teacher. We are here together.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr schön! Ihr seid alle sehr nett.
lines.en: Very nice! You are all very kind.

days.title: Das Verb haben im Präsens
days.objective: Use all present-tense forms of haben to talk about what people have and about physical states like hunger and thirst.
days.paretoFocus: haben is the second pillar of German after sein, and German expresses hunger, thirst, luck and time with haben where English uses to be.
drills: Recite the haben paradigm out loud, stressing the missing b in hast and hat.
Write four sentences about states you feel today using Hunger, Durst, Zeit and Glück.
Turn each of your sentences into a question by moving the verb to the front.

items: Which two forms of haben lose the b of the stem?
How does German say 'He is thirsty' literally?
Which form of haben looks exactly like the infinitive?

activities.title: Ich habe, du hast, er hat
activities.intro: haben is regular apart from two forms: du hast and er, sie, es hat both drop the b of the stem. Everything else follows the ordinary pattern. German also uses haben for many states that English builds with to be.

items.en: I have time.
items.note: No article is needed with Zeit here.

items.en: You have a fever.
items.note: hast, not habst, because the b disappears.

items.en: She is thirsty.
items.note: Literally she has thirst.

items.en: We are lucky.
items.note: haben is identical to the infinitive in wir haben, sie haben and Sie haben.

items.en: You have holidays.
items.note: ihr keeps the b and adds t.

activities.title: Formen von haben
activities.intro: Practise the present tense of haben, especially the two forms that drop the b.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Er ___ heute frei.
questions.explanation: er, sie and es take hat, which loses the b of the stem.

questions.prompt: Which line uses haben correctly with wir?
questions.explanation: wir takes the form haben, which is identical to the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right form of haben: Ihr ___ Glück.
questions.explanation: ihr keeps the b of the stem and adds the ending t.

questions.prompt: Complete this sentence about being free today: Ich ___ heute Zeit.
questions.explanation: ich takes the ending e on the full stem hab.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Do you have time?' spoken to one friend.
questions.explanation: In a yes or no question the conjugated verb comes first and the subject follows it. Finish the written sentence with a question mark.

activities.title: Hunger und Durst
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks whether you are free and hungry, and every line uses a form of haben.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hallo Sofia, hast du heute Zeit?
lines.en: Hello Sofia, do you have time today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe Zeit.
lines.en: Yes, I have time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich habe Hunger. Und du?
lines.en: I am hungry. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe auch Hunger und Durst.
lines.en: I am hungry and thirsty too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir haben Glück, heute ist Markt.
lines.en: We are lucky, there is a market today.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gut! Habt ihr auch Zeit?
lines.en: Very good! Do you have time as well?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, wir haben alle Zeit.
lines.en: Yes, we all have time.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Anna hat heute leider Ferien.
lines.en: Unfortunately Anna is on holiday today.

days.title: Regelmäßige Verben im Präsens
days.objective: Conjugate any regular German verb in the present tense by adding the six standard endings to its stem.
days.paretoFocus: The great majority of German verbs are regular, so one small ending table unlocks thousands of verbs at once.
drills: Take the verbs lernen, wohnen, spielen and machen and write all six forms of each.
Say what you do every week in five sentences, changing the subject each time.
Practise the extra e in du arbeitest and er arbeitet until it sounds automatic.

items: What are the six present-tense endings in order from ich to sie?
Which two forms of a regular verb are always identical to each other?
Why does arbeiten need an extra e before the du and er endings?

activities.title: Endungen im Präsens
activities.intro: To conjugate a regular verb, take the infinitive, remove the final en to get the stem, then add e, st, t, en, t, en. The wir form and the sie form always end in en, exactly like the infinitive. If the stem ends in t or d, an extra e is inserted so the ending stays pronounceable.

items.en: I am learning German.
items.note: lernen minus en gives the stem lern, plus e.

items.en: You live in Berlin.
items.note: The du ending is st.

items.en: He plays football.
items.note: ß follows the long vowel in Fußball.

items.en: We work a lot.
items.note: Stems in t or d insert e: du arbeitest, er arbeitet.

items.en: You are taking a break.
items.note: The ihr ending is t, the same letter as the er ending.

activities.title: Verben richtig bilden
activities.intro: Build regular present-tense forms from the stem and the correct ending.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Wir ___ jeden Tag Deutsch.
questions.explanation: wir takes the ending en, so the form looks identical to the infinitive lernen.

questions.prompt: Which form of wohnen belongs to du?
questions.explanation: The du ending is st, added straight onto the stem wohn.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of spielen: Er ___ gern Fußball.
questions.explanation: er, sie and es take the ending t on the stem spiel.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of arbeiten: Du ___ heute viel.
questions.explanation: The stem arbeit ends in t, so an e is inserted before the du ending st.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We work a lot.'
questions.explanation: Subject first, conjugated verb second, and the adverb after the verb.

activities.title: Was machst du?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Two learners compare their daily routines using regular verbs only.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was machst du heute, Sofia?
lines.en: What are you doing today, Sofia?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich lerne Deutsch und ich arbeite ein bisschen.
lines.en: I am learning German and I am working a little.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo arbeitest du?
lines.en: Where do you work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich arbeite zu Hause. Und du?
lines.en: I work at home. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich spiele Fußball und ich lerne auch.
lines.en: I play football and I study too.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Lernst du Spanisch?
lines.en: Are you learning Spanish?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, wir lernen zusammen Spanisch.
lines.en: Yes, we learn Spanish together.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gern, ihr macht das gut.
lines.en: With pleasure, you are doing that well.

days.title: Das Verb an zweiter Stelle
days.objective: Start a statement with a time or place expression and still keep the conjugated verb in second position.
days.paretoFocus: Verb second is the backbone of German word order, and getting it wrong is the single most audible learner mistake.
drills: Take five sentences you already know and rewrite each one starting with a time expression.
Read the rewritten sentences aloud and tap the table on the verb to feel that it is always the second element.
Write three sentences beginning with Hier, Jetzt and Morgen, checking that the subject sits right after the verb.

items: In a German statement, which element always occupies position two?
What happens to the subject when a time expression opens the sentence?
Does a two word phrase like Heute Abend count as one position or two?

activities.title: Die zweite Position
activities.intro: In a German statement the conjugated verb is nailed to position two. Position one can be filled by the subject, but it can just as easily be an expression of time or place. When something other than the subject goes first, the subject simply moves behind the verb so that the verb still counts as the second element.

items.en: Today I am learning German.
items.note: Heute takes position one, so ich moves behind lerne.

items.en: Anna is coming tomorrow.
items.note: The verb stays second, the subject Anna follows it.

items.en: Now we are playing football.
items.note: Never say Jetzt wir spielen.

items.en: Thomas lives here.
items.note: A place expression can also open the sentence.

items.en: This evening we are working together.
items.note: A whole phrase counts as one single position.

activities.title: Wortstellung üben
activities.intro: Check that the conjugated verb sits in second position in every statement.

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the verb correctly after an opening time word?
questions.explanation: Heute fills position one, so the verb lerne must come next and the subject follows it.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word order with Jetzt at the front:
questions.explanation: Only one element may stand before the verb, so wir has to move behind spielen.

questions.prompt: Put the verb wohnen in the right place and form: Hier ___ Thomas.
questions.explanation: The place word Hier is position one, so the verb form for Thomas follows immediately.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb kommen so it stays in second position: Morgen ___ Anna.
questions.explanation: Morgen occupies position one and the conjugated verb for Anna takes position two.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'This evening we are working together.'
questions.explanation: The time phrase counts as a single first element, so the verb comes next and the subject after it.

activities.title: Heute und morgen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Two friends compare plans, and almost every line begins with a time expression.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Heute arbeite ich viel. Und du?
lines.en: Today I am working a lot. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Heute lerne ich Deutsch.
lines.en: Today I am learning German.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Morgen kommt Anna aus Hamburg.
lines.en: Anna is coming from Hamburg tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super! Morgen spielen wir zusammen Fußball.
lines.en: Great! Tomorrow we are playing football together.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jetzt habe ich leider wenig Zeit.
lines.en: Right now I unfortunately have little time.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alles klar, heute Abend lerne ich auch.
lines.en: All right, this evening I am studying as well.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hier arbeitet ihr sehr gut zusammen.
lines.en: You work together very well here.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke! Morgen sind wir wieder hier.
lines.en: Thanks! Tomorrow we are here again.

days.title: Ja-Nein-Fragen und W-Fragen
days.objective: Ask both yes or no questions and information questions with the correct position of the verb.
days.paretoFocus: Questions are how a beginner keeps a conversation alive, and German only needs two simple word order patterns to build all of them.
drills: Turn five statements you already know into yes or no questions by moving the verb to the front.
Write one question each with Wer, Was, Wo, Woher and Wie about a person you know.
Practise saying a yes or no question with rising intonation and a W question with falling intonation.

items: Where does the conjugated verb stand in a yes or no question?
Which question word asks about origin, and which asks about a current location?
Why does the verb still count as the second element in a W question?

activities.title: Fragen stellen
activities.intro: A yes or no question simply moves the conjugated verb to position one, ahead of the subject. A W question starts with a question word such as Wer, Was, Wo, Woher, Wann or Wie, and that word fills position one, so the verb still lands in position two. German never needs a helper word like do.

items.en: Do you come from Germany?
items.note: The verb jumps in front of the subject.

items.en: Do you live in Hamburg?
items.note: No helper verb, just verb then subject.

items.en: What is your name?
items.note: heißt is written with ß because ei is a diphthong.

items.en: Where do you live?
items.note: Wo asks about a position, Wohin about a direction.

items.en: Where does Anna come from?
items.note: Woher asks about origin.

activities.title: Fragewörter und Wortstellung
activities.intro: Choose the right question word and put the verb in the right slot.

questions.prompt: Which question word asks about a place?
questions.explanation: Wo asks about a place. Wie asks how, Wer asks which person, Wann asks about time.

questions.prompt: Choose the correctly built yes or no question:
questions.explanation: The conjugated verb takes position one and keeps the du ending st, with the subject right behind it.

questions.prompt: Ask someone for their name. Complete: ___ heißt du?
questions.explanation: German asks about a name with the word for how, not with the word for what.

questions.prompt: Ask about someone's country of origin. Complete: ___ kommst du?
questions.explanation: This question word asks where someone comes from, so it pairs with kommen and aus.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Where do you live?' spoken to one friend.
questions.explanation: The question word fills position one, so the verb is second and the subject third.

activities.title: Fragen und Antworten
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone interviews you at a course sign up desk using both question types.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie heißt du?
lines.en: What is your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich heiße Sofia.
lines.en: My name is Sofia.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Woher kommst du?
lines.en: Where do you come from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich komme aus Spanien. Und du?
lines.en: I come from Spain. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich komme aus Deutschland. Wo wohnst du jetzt?
lines.en: I come from Germany. Where do you live now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich wohne in Berlin.
lines.en: I live in Berlin.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Arbeitest du hier?
lines.en: Do you work here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich arbeite hier und ich lerne Deutsch.
lines.en: Yes, I work here and I am learning German.

days.title: Der bestimmte Artikel und das Genus
days.objective: Use der, die and das correctly with common nouns and recognise the endings that predict a noun's gender.
days.paretoFocus: Every German noun carries a gender, and learning the article together with the word is the one habit that prevents years of guessing.
drills: Label ten objects around you with a sticky note showing the article and the noun together.
Sort twenty known nouns into three columns headed der, die and das.
Say each noun aloud as one single unit, for example dieLampe, so the article becomes part of the word.

items: Which article goes with Fenster, and which goes with Lampe?
What gender is typical for nouns that end in e?
Which ending always makes a noun neuter, even when it refers to a person?

activities.title: Der, die, das
activities.intro: German nouns are masculine, feminine or neuter, and the definite article shows which one. Gender is grammatical rather than logical, so it must be learned with the word. A few endings do give reliable hints, and those are worth memorising early. Every German noun is written with a capital letter.

items.en: The table is new.
items.note: Learn nouns as der Tisch, never as Tisch alone.

items.en: The lamp is beautiful.
items.note: Nouns ending in e are very often feminine.

items.en: The window is open.
items.note: Many nouns in er that are not people are neuter.

items.en: The door is old.
items.note: This one has no hint, so it has to be memorised.

items.en: The girl is friendly.
items.note: Nouns ending in chen are always neuter, whatever they mean.

activities.title: Genus erkennen
activities.intro: Match nouns to their definite article and spot the gender hints.

questions.prompt: Which article belongs to Fenster?
questions.explanation: Fenster is neuter, so it takes das.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is feminine?
questions.explanation: Lampe ends in e and is feminine, so it takes die. Tisch and Stuhl are masculine and Fenster is neuter.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct definite article: ___ Stuhl ist bequem.
questions.explanation: Stuhl is masculine, so the article at the start of the sentence is written with a capital letter.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct definite article: ___ Tür ist alt.
questions.explanation: Tür is feminine and the article opens the sentence, so it is capitalised.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The window is open.'
questions.explanation: Article and noun form the subject in position one, and the verb follows in position two.

activities.title: Das Zimmer
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend shows you a room and you describe the furniture with the right articles.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hier ist der Tisch. Ist der Tisch neu?
lines.en: Here is the table. Is the table new?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, der Tisch ist neu.
lines.en: Yes, the table is new.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Lampe? Ist die Lampe auch neu?
lines.en: And the lamp? Is the lamp new as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, die Lampe ist alt.
lines.en: No, the lamp is old.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das Fenster ist sehr groß.
lines.en: The window is very big.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, und die Tür ist klein.
lines.en: Yes, and the door is small.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie ist das Zimmer?
lines.en: What is the room like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das Zimmer ist wirklich schön.
lines.en: The room is really beautiful.

days.title: Der unbestimmte Artikel ein und eine
days.objective: Introduce a new thing with ein or eine and choose between the indefinite and the definite article correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Naming and identifying objects is the first thing a beginner does in a shop, a flat or a classroom, and it always starts with ein or eine.
drills: Point at ten objects and say Das ist ein or Das ist eine plus the noun.
Say each object twice: first with the indefinite article, then with the definite article.
Write five yes or no questions of the pattern Ist das ein plus a noun.

items: Which two genders share the form ein?
How do you say 'That is a lamp' when the lamp is new to the conversation?
When do you switch from ein to der, die or das?

activities.title: Ein oder eine
activities.intro: The indefinite article has only two forms in the nominative: ein for masculine and neuter nouns, and eine for feminine nouns. Use it when the thing is new in the conversation. Once everybody knows which thing you mean, switch to der, die or das.

items.en: That is a table.
items.note: Masculine der Tisch becomes ein Tisch.

items.en: That is a lamp.
items.note: Feminine die Lampe becomes eine Lampe.

items.en: That is a window.
items.note: Neuter das Fenster also takes ein.

items.en: Here is a door.
items.note: The verb stays second even after Hier.

items.en: Is that a car?
items.note: After the answer you would continue with das Auto, because it is now known.

activities.title: Ein und eine üben
activities.intro: Pick the indefinite article that matches the gender of the noun.

questions.prompt: Choose the right indefinite article: Das ist ___ Lampe.
questions.explanation: Lampe is feminine, so it takes eine in the nominative.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns takes ein rather than eine?
questions.explanation: Fenster is neuter and neuter nouns take ein. The other three are feminine.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right indefinite article: Hier ist ___ Tür.
questions.explanation: Tür is feminine, so the indefinite article carries the ending e.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right indefinite article: Ist das ___ Auto?
questions.explanation: Auto is neuter, so the indefinite article has no ending.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'That is a table.'
questions.explanation: Das is the subject, ist is the verb in position two, and the article stands directly before the noun.

activities.title: Was ist das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend points at objects in a shop and you name each one with ein or eine.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was ist das?
lines.en: What is that?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist ein Tisch.
lines.en: That is a table.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was ist das hier?
lines.en: And what is this here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das ist eine Lampe.
lines.en: That is a lamp.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist das ein Fenster?
lines.en: Is that a window?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, das ist eine Tür.
lines.en: No, that is a door.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und da? Ist das ein Auto?
lines.en: And over there? Is that a car?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, das ist ein Auto. Das Auto ist neu.
lines.en: Yes, that is a car. The car is new.

days.title: Der Plural der Substantive
days.objective: Form and use the plural of common nouns and remember that every plural takes the article die.
days.paretoFocus: German plural endings are unpredictable, so learning each noun with its plural from the start saves an enormous amount of relearning later.
drills: Write ten known nouns in three columns: singular with article, plural with article, and plural ending.
Say each pair aloud as a chant, for example der Tisch, die Tische.
Count objects around you out loud from one to five, changing the noun into the plural.

items: Which article do all plural nouns take, regardless of gender?
What is the plural of das Buch and what changes inside the word?
Which kind of noun usually forms its plural with s?

activities.title: Singular und Plural
activities.intro: In the plural all three genders collapse into one article: die. The ending itself varies and cannot be predicted reliably, so every noun should be learned together with its plural form. Some nouns also add an Umlaut to the stem vowel.

items.en: The table, the tables.
items.note: Many masculine nouns add e.

items.en: The lamp, the lamps.
items.note: Most feminine nouns add n or en.

items.en: The window, the windows.
items.note: Here nothing is added, only the article changes.

items.en: The book, the books.
items.note: The stem vowel takes an Umlaut and the ending is er.

items.en: The car, the cars.
items.note: Words borrowed from other languages often add s.

activities.title: Pluralformen
activities.intro: Build plural forms and remember which article goes with them.

questions.prompt: What is the plural of das Buch?
questions.explanation: This noun adds the ending er and puts an Umlaut on the stem vowel.

questions.prompt: Which article stands in front of every plural noun?
questions.explanation: In the plural, gender disappears and all nouns take die.

questions.prompt: Write the plural of the noun Lampe together with its article.
questions.explanation: Feminine nouns ending in e usually add n, and the plural article is always die.

questions.prompt: Give the plural of der Tisch together with its article.
questions.explanation: This masculine noun adds e in the plural and switches its article to die.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The books are new.'
questions.explanation: A plural subject needs the plural form sind of the verb sein.

activities.title: Wie viele sind hier?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague count the furniture in a room, so nearly every noun is plural.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie viele Stühle sind hier?
lines.en: How many chairs are here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hier sind vier Stühle.
lines.en: There are four chairs here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Tische? Wie viele Tische sind da?
lines.en: And the tables? How many tables are there?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da sind zwei Tische.
lines.en: There are two tables there.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sind die Fenster groß?
lines.en: Are the windows big?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, die Fenster sind sehr groß.
lines.en: Yes, the windows are very big.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo sind die Bücher?
lines.en: Where are the books?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Bücher sind hier, und die Lampen sind auch hier.
lines.en: The books are here, and the lamps are here too.

days.title: Der Akkusativ, den, die, das
days.objective: Use the accusative definite articles den, die and das for the object of a sentence.
days.paretoFocus: The accusative is the first case a learner needs, and only one article actually changes, so the payoff is huge for very little memorisation.
drills: Write five sentences with Ich sehe and a masculine noun, checking the article each time.
Take five nominative sentences and rewrite them as objects after Ich nehme.
Say out loud ten times: der becomes den, everything else stays the same.

items: Which single gender changes its definite article in the accusative?
What is the accusative article for a feminine noun?
Does the plural article change when the noun becomes the object?

activities.title: Nominativ und Akkusativ
activities.intro: The subject of a sentence stands in the nominative and the direct object stands in the accusative. The good news is that only masculine nouns change: der becomes den. Feminine die, neuter das and plural die look exactly the same as in the nominative.

items.en: I see the table.
items.note: Masculine der Tisch becomes den Tisch as an object.

items.en: I see the lamp.
items.note: Feminine die does not change at all.

items.en: I see the window.
items.note: Neuter das does not change either.

items.en: I see the books.
items.note: The plural article stays die.

items.hr: Der Stuhl ist neu. Ich nehme den Stuhl.
items.en: The chair is new. I am taking the chair.
items.note: Same noun, subject first, then object with den.

activities.title: Den, die oder das
activities.intro: Choose the correct accusative article for each direct object.

questions.prompt: Choose the accusative article: Ich sehe ___ Tisch.
questions.explanation: Tisch is masculine, and masculine der changes to den when the noun is the direct object.

questions.prompt: Which gender changes its definite article in the accusative?
questions.explanation: Only masculine changes, from der to den. The other three keep their nominative form.

questions.prompt: Complete the object with the right accusative article: Ich nehme ___ Stuhl.
questions.explanation: Stuhl is masculine and stands as the direct object, so the article gains an n.

questions.prompt: Complete the object with the right accusative article: Ich sehe ___ Lampe.
questions.explanation: Lampe is feminine, and feminine articles are identical in the nominative and the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I see the window.'
questions.explanation: Subject, verb in position two, then the object with its unchanged neuter article.

activities.title: Was siehst du?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend quizzes you about what you can see in a room, so every answer contains an accusative object.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was siehst du?
lines.en: What do you see?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich sehe den Tisch und die Lampe.
lines.en: I see the table and the lamp.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Siehst du auch das Fenster?
lines.en: Do you also see the window?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich sehe das Fenster.
lines.en: Yes, I see the window.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Bücher? Siehst du die Bücher?
lines.en: And the books? Do you see the books?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich sehe die Bücher auch.
lines.en: Yes, I see the books too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nimmst du den Stuhl?
lines.en: Are you taking the chair?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich nehme den Stuhl.
lines.en: Yes, I am taking the chair.

days.title: Der Akkusativ nach haben, kaufen und brauchen
days.objective: Use einen, eine and ein correctly after the everyday verbs haben, kaufen and brauchen.
days.paretoFocus: These three verbs cover shopping, describing possessions and stating needs, which is most of what a beginner has to say in a real transaction.
drills: Write five sentences beginning with Ich brauche, each with a different gender of noun.
Say what you own and what you still need, using haben and brauchen alternately.
Drill the pair ein and einen out loud until the masculine form comes automatically.

items: What happens to ein when the noun is masculine and the object of the sentence?
How do you say 'We are buying a table' in German?
Do feminine and neuter indefinite articles change in the accusative?

activities.title: Einen, eine, ein
activities.intro: haben, kaufen and brauchen always take a direct object, so the noun after them stands in the accusative. The indefinite article follows the same rule as the definite one: only masculine changes, and ein becomes einen. Feminine eine and neuter ein stay exactly as they are.

items.en: I have a brother.
items.note: Masculine der Bruder gives einen Bruder in the accusative.

items.en: We are buying a table.
items.note: Same masculine ending after kaufen.

items.en: She needs a lamp.
items.note: Feminine eine is unchanged.

items.en: He has a car.
items.note: Neuter ein is unchanged too.

items.en: Are you buying the books?
items.note: Plural has no indefinite article, so you either use die or nothing at all.

activities.title: Objekte im Akkusativ
activities.intro: Choose the right indefinite article for the object after haben, kaufen and brauchen.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct object form: Wir kaufen ___ Tisch.
questions.explanation: Tisch is masculine and stands as the direct object, so the indefinite article takes the ending en.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses brauchen with the correct article?
questions.explanation: Lampe is feminine, and the feminine indefinite article is the same in the nominative and the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete the object after haben: Ich habe ___ Bruder.
questions.explanation: Bruder is masculine, so the indefinite article gains the accusative ending en.

questions.prompt: Complete the object after haben: Er hat ___ Auto.
questions.explanation: Auto is neuter, and neuter indefinite articles never change in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'She needs a lamp.'
questions.explanation: Subject, then the verb in position two, then the accusative object with its feminine article.

activities.title: Wir kaufen Möbel
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a flatmate work through a shopping list, so every line has an accusative object.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wir brauchen einen Tisch. Kaufen wir einen Tisch?
lines.en: We need a table. Shall we buy a table?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich kaufe einen Tisch.
lines.en: Yes, I am buying a table.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Brauchst du auch eine Lampe?
lines.en: Do you need a lamp too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich brauche eine Lampe.
lines.en: Yes, I need a lamp.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du schon ein Auto?
lines.en: Do you already have a car?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe ein Auto.
lines.en: Yes, I have a car.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und Bücher? Kaufst du auch Bücher?
lines.en: And books? Are you buying books too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich kaufe die Bücher hier.
lines.en: Yes, I am buying the books here.

days.title: Die Negation mit nicht und kein
days.objective: Say no in German by negating a verb or an adjective with nicht and a noun with the right form of kein.
days.paretoFocus: Learners need to say what they do not have and do not do from the very first week, and German splits that job between two different words.
drills: Take five true sentences about yourself and make each one negative.
Sort ten sentences into two groups: those that need nicht and those that need kein.
Practise the three forms kein, keine and keinen out loud with a matching noun each time.

items: Which word negates a noun that would otherwise take ein or no article?
Where does nicht usually stand when it negates the whole sentence?
What is the masculine accusative form of kein?

activities.title: Nicht oder kein
activities.intro: German has two negation words. Use kein before a noun that would otherwise carry ein, eine or no article at all. Use nicht for everything else, above all for verbs, adjectives and nouns that already carry a definite article. kein takes exactly the same endings as ein.

items.en: I am not working today.
items.note: nicht stands at the end when it negates the whole statement.

items.en: That is not new.
items.note: nicht stands directly before an adjective.

items.en: I do not have a car.
items.note: Neuter kein, because you would otherwise say ein Auto.

items.en: We do not have time.
items.note: Feminine keine, even though Zeit normally has no article.

items.en: He does not have a brother.
items.note: Masculine accusative takes the ending en, exactly like einen.

activities.title: Verneinen üben
activities.intro: Decide between nicht and the correct form of kein.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct negation: Ich habe ___ Zeit.
questions.explanation: Zeit is a feminine noun with no article, so it is negated with the feminine form of kein.

questions.prompt: When do you use kein instead of nicht?
options: before a noun that would take ein or no article
always at the end of the sentence
questions.answer: before a noun that would take ein or no article
questions.explanation: kein replaces the indefinite article or a missing article. Verbs and adjectives are negated with nicht.

questions.prompt: Negate the object: Er hat ___ Bruder.
questions.explanation: Bruder is masculine and is the direct object, so the negation word carries the accusative ending en.

questions.prompt: Negate this whole statement: Ich arbeite heute ___.
questions.explanation: There is no noun to negate here, only the activity, so the sentence negation word goes at the end.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I do not have a car.'
questions.explanation: The negation word replaces the indefinite article and stands directly in front of the neuter noun.

activities.title: Nein, leider nicht
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone asks you a series of questions and you answer no every time, alternating between nicht and kein.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du ein Auto?
lines.en: Do you have a car?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich habe kein Auto.
lines.en: No, I do not have a car.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Arbeitest du heute?
lines.en: Are you working today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, heute arbeite ich nicht.
lines.en: No, today I am not working.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du jetzt Zeit?
lines.en: Do you have time now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich habe leider keine Zeit.
lines.en: No, unfortunately I do not have time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist das Buch neu?
lines.en: Is the book new?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, das Buch ist nicht neu.
lines.en: No, the book is not new.

days.title: Possessivartikel im Nominativ
days.objective: Say whose something is with mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser and euer in subject position.
days.paretoFocus: Possessives appear in almost every sentence about family, home and belongings, and they reuse the ein endings you already know.
drills: Write six sentences, one for each possessive word, describing a person or an object.
Say five things in your room with the word for my, adding the e where the noun is feminine or plural.
Practise the contrast between sein and ihr by describing a man and a woman in turn.

items: Which possessive words take an extra e in the nominative, and with which nouns?
What is the difference in meaning between sein and ihr?
How do you know from writing alone whether Ihr means their or the polite your?

activities.title: Mein, dein, sein, ihr
activities.intro: The possessive words behave exactly like ein. In the nominative they have no ending before masculine and neuter nouns, and they add e before feminine and plural nouns. The word for his is sein and the word for her is ihr, and both refer to the owner, not to the object.

items.en: My brother lives in Berlin.
items.note: No ending before a masculine noun.

items.en: My sister is a teacher.
items.note: The ending e appears before a feminine noun.

items.en: Your car is new.
items.note: Neuter nouns take the bare form, like masculine ones.

items.en: His father works a lot.
items.note: sein means his, ihr means her.

items.en: Our parents are nice.
items.note: Plural nouns always take the ending e.

activities.title: Wem gehört das?
activities.intro: Choose the possessive word with the correct ending in subject position.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct possessive: ___ Schwester ist Lehrerin.
questions.explanation: Schwester is feminine, so the possessive takes the ending e, just like eine.

questions.prompt: Which word means his?
questions.explanation: sein means his, ihr means her or their, dein means your and unser means our.

questions.prompt: Use the informal word for your: ___ Auto ist neu.
questions.explanation: Auto is neuter, so the informal possessive has no ending, and it is capitalised at the start of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Use the word for our: ___ Eltern sind nett.
questions.explanation: Eltern is plural, so the possessive takes the ending e.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My brother is a teacher.'
questions.explanation: The possessive takes no ending before a masculine noun, and a profession after sein needs no article.

activities.title: Meine Leute
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A new colleague asks about the people in your life and every answer starts from a possessive word.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo wohnt dein Bruder?
lines.en: Where does your brother live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mein Bruder wohnt in Berlin.
lines.en: My brother lives in Berlin.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und deine Schwester?
lines.en: And your sister?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Meine Schwester ist Lehrerin. Sie wohnt in Hamburg.
lines.en: My sister is a teacher. She lives in Hamburg.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sind eure Eltern auch hier?
lines.en: Are your parents here too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, unsere Eltern wohnen in Spanien.
lines.en: No, our parents live in Spain.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist dein Vater auch Lehrer?
lines.en: Is your father a teacher as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, mein Vater ist Arzt.
lines.en: No, my father is a doctor.

days.title: Possessivartikel im Akkusativ
days.objective: Use possessive words as the object of a sentence, adding the ending en before masculine nouns.
days.paretoFocus: Talking about visiting, seeing or taking someone else's things is everyday speech, and it requires the one accusative ending that learners most often forget.
drills: Rewrite five subject sentences with possessives so that the possessive phrase becomes the object.
Write four sentences with the verb besuchen and a different family member each time.
Say aloud the pair mein Bruder and meinen Bruder until the difference is automatic.

items: Which ending does a possessive word take before a masculine object?
Do feminine, neuter and plural possessives change in the accusative?
How would you say 'Are you visiting your father?' to one friend?

activities.title: Meinen, deinen, unseren
activities.intro: When a possessive phrase becomes the direct object, the same rule applies as with ein and kein. Only the masculine form changes, adding the ending en. Feminine, neuter and plural forms look exactly as they do in the nominative.

items.en: I see my brother.
items.note: Masculine object, so the ending en appears.

items.en: I see my sister.
items.note: Feminine keeps the ending e.

items.en: I have my car here.
items.note: Neuter keeps the bare form.

items.en: We are visiting our parents.
items.note: Plural keeps the ending e.

items.en: Are you seeing your father?
items.note: Masculine object again, so the informal possessive ends in en.

activities.title: Possessivartikel als Objekt
activities.intro: Put possessive words into the accusative and watch the masculine ending.

questions.prompt: Choose the accusative possessive: Ich sehe ___ Bruder.
questions.explanation: Bruder is masculine and is the direct object, so the possessive takes the accusative ending en.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct accusative sentence about parents:
questions.explanation: Eltern is plural, and plural possessives keep the ending e in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Use the informal possessive in the accusative: Siehst du ___ Vater?
questions.explanation: Vater is masculine and is the object of the question, so the possessive ends in en.

questions.prompt: Use the possessive that means my: Ich habe ___ Auto hier.
questions.explanation: Auto is neuter, and neuter possessives are identical in the nominative and the accusative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I see my sister.'
questions.explanation: The object follows the verb and the feminine possessive keeps its ending e.

activities.title: Ich besuche meinen Bruder
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain your plans for the day and every plan involves visiting or taking something of your own.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was machst du heute Nachmittag?
lines.en: What are you doing this afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich besuche meinen Bruder.
lines.en: I am visiting my brother.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Siehst du auch deine Schwester?
lines.en: Are you seeing your sister too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich sehe meine Schwester heute Abend.
lines.en: Yes, I am seeing my sister this evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Besucht ihr eure Eltern?
lines.en: Are you visiting your parents?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, wir besuchen unsere Eltern morgen.
lines.en: Yes, we are visiting our parents tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nimmst du dein Auto?
lines.en: Are you taking your car?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich nehme mein Auto.
lines.en: Yes, I am taking my car.

days.title: Die Familie beschreiben
days.objective: Describe your own family in several connected sentences using family vocabulary, possessives and the accusative.
days.paretoFocus: Describing your family is one of the most predictable topics in any first conversation and in every beginner speaking test.
drills: Draw a simple family tree and label every person with the article and the noun.
Speak for one minute about your family without stopping, even if you repeat yourself.
Write four sentences about relatives you do not have, using the right form of kein.

items: Name the six closest family members with their articles.
How do you say that you have one brother and one sister in a single sentence?
Which possessive ending do you need before the plural word for parents?

activities.title: Meine Familie
activities.intro: Family vocabulary combines everything from the last few lessons: articles, plurals, possessives and the accusative after haben. Learn each family word together with its article, because the gender decides every ending around it. Two clauses can be joined with und without changing the word order.

items.en: My family is small.
items.note: die Familie is feminine, so the possessive takes e.

items.hr: Ich habe einen Bruder und eine Schwester.
items.en: I have one brother and one sister.
items.note: der Bruder gives einen, die Schwester gives eine.

items.hr: Mein Vater heißt Thomas und meine Mutter heißt Anna.
items.en: My father is called Thomas and my mother is called Anna.
items.note: und simply joins two full sentences, and each verb stays in position two.

items.en: My grandparents live in Hamburg.
items.note: die Großeltern is plural only, so the verb is plural too.

items.en: We do not have children.
items.note: das Kind, die Kinder, and the plural negation is keine.

activities.title: Familienwörter
activities.intro: Practise family vocabulary together with the endings you have already learned.

questions.prompt: Which German word means grandmother?
questions.explanation: die Tante is aunt, die Schwester is sister and die Tochter is daughter.

questions.prompt: Which family word is feminine?
questions.explanation: Only die Tante takes die. The other three are masculine and take der.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right form of sein: Meine Familie ___ klein.
questions.explanation: Familie is a singular noun, so the verb takes the third person singular form.

questions.prompt: Complete with the German word for parents: Meine ___ wohnen in Hamburg.
questions.explanation: This noun exists only in the plural, which is why the verb also carries the plural ending.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have a brother and a sister.'
questions.explanation: Both nouns are objects of haben, so the masculine one takes einen and the feminine one keeps eine.

activities.title: Ist deine Familie groß?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone at a party asks about your family and you answer with full sentences.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist deine Familie groß?
lines.en: Is your family big?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, meine Familie ist klein.
lines.en: No, my family is small.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du Geschwister?
lines.en: Do you have siblings?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe einen Bruder und eine Schwester.
lines.en: Yes, I have a brother and a sister.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie heißen deine Eltern?
lines.en: What are your parents called?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mein Vater heißt Thomas und meine Mutter heißt Anna.
lines.en: My father is called Thomas and my mother is called Anna.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo wohnen deine Großeltern?
lines.en: Where do your grandparents live?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Meine Großeltern wohnen in Hamburg.
lines.en: My grandparents live in Hamburg.

days.title: Verben mit Vokalwechsel a zu ä
days.objective: Conjugate strong verbs such as fahren, schlafen and tragen, changing the stem vowel a to ä in the du and er forms.
days.paretoFocus: Several of the most common everyday verbs belong to this group, and using the plain a where an Umlaut is required is instantly recognisable as a mistake.
drills: Write out all six forms of fahren, schlafen and tragen, underlining every Umlaut.
Say five sentences about travel plans, changing the subject between ich, du and er.
Check any new verb you meet: if the stem has an a, test whether the du form takes an Umlaut.

items: In which two persons does the stem vowel change?
What is the er form of tragen?
Which verb changes au to äu instead of a to ä?

activities.title: Ich fahre, du fährst
activities.intro: A group of very common verbs changes the stem vowel a into ä, but only in the du form and in the er, sie, es form. All other persons keep the plain a and the ordinary endings. A few verbs with au change it to äu in exactly the same two places.

items.hr: Ich fahre nach Berlin, aber er fährt nach Hamburg.
items.en: I am going to Berlin, but he is going to Hamburg.
items.note: ich keeps a, er takes ä.

items.en: You sleep for a long time.
items.note: schlafen becomes du schläfst and er schläft.

items.en: She is carrying a bag.
items.note: tragen becomes du trägst and sie trägt.

items.hr: Wir fahren zusammen und ihr fahrt auch.
items.en: We are travelling together and you are travelling too.
items.note: wir and ihr never take the Umlaut.

items.en: He runs very fast.
items.note: laufen changes au to äu in the same two persons.

activities.title: Vokalwechsel üben
activities.intro: Decide where the stem vowel takes an Umlaut and where it stays plain.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Er ___ nach Hamburg.
questions.explanation: The third person singular takes the Umlaut and the ending t.

questions.prompt: In which persons does the stem vowel take an Umlaut?
questions.explanation: The change is limited to the second and third person singular. Every other form keeps the plain vowel.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of schlafen: Du ___ lange.
questions.explanation: The du form takes the Umlaut and the ending st.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of tragen: Sie ___ eine Tasche.
questions.explanation: The third person singular takes the Umlaut, and the object keeps the feminine article eine.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'He runs fast.'
questions.explanation: This verb changes au to äu in the third person singular, and the verb stays in position two.

activities.title: Wohin fährst du?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend talk about a trip tomorrow, so the vowel changing verbs appear in every line.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wohin fährst du morgen?
lines.en: Where are you going tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich fahre nach Hamburg. Und du?
lines.en: I am going to Hamburg. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich fahre nach Berlin. Fährt Anna auch?
lines.en: I am going to Berlin. Is Anna going too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, Anna fährt auch nach Berlin.
lines.en: Yes, Anna is going to Berlin as well.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schläfst du morgen lange?
lines.en: Are you sleeping late tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, morgen schlafe ich kurz.
lines.en: No, tomorrow I am sleeping only a short time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trägst du viel Gepäck?
lines.en: Are you carrying a lot of luggage?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich trage nur eine Tasche.
lines.en: No, I am only carrying one bag.

days.title: Verben mit Vokalwechsel e zu i und e zu ie
days.objective: Conjugate the common e to i and e to ie verbs correctly in the du and er/sie/es forms.
days.paretoFocus: The verbs that change their stem vowel are the ones you use every hour: speak, read, take, see, eat, so getting them right immediately makes your German sound native rather than textbook.
drills: Say five sentences about a friend using sprechen, lesen, nehmen, sehen and essen in the er/sie form.
Turn each of those sentences into a du question and answer it about yourself.
Write the full present tense of nehmen from memory, then check where the stem vowel changed.

items: Which two forms of a vowel-changing verb are the only ones that change?
What is the du form of lesen, and what is the er form?
Say what you eat for breakfast and what your partner eats.

activities.title: Der Vokal wechselt
activities.intro: A small group of German verbs changes its stem vowel from e to i or from e to ie, but only in the second and third person singular. Every other form keeps the plain stem. The endings themselves never change, so once you know the new stem you already know the form.

items.hr: Ich spreche Deutsch, und du sprichst Englisch.
items.en: I speak German, and you speak English.
items.note: sprechen: du sprichst, er spricht (e becomes i)

items.hr: Er liest die Zeitung am Morgen.
items.en: He reads the newspaper in the morning.
items.note: lesen: du liest, er liest (e becomes ie)

items.hr: Nimmst du den Bus oder das Fahrrad?
items.en: Are you taking the bus or the bike?
items.note: nehmen: du nimmst, er nimmt; the stem also loses the h

items.hr: Sie sieht heute Abend einen Film.
items.en: She is watching a film this evening.
items.note: sehen: du siehst, sie sieht

items.hr: Was isst du gern zum Frühstück?
items.en: What do you like to eat for breakfast?
items.note: essen: du isst, er isst; short vowel, so ss and not ß

activities.title: Stamm und Endung
activities.intro: Practise the du and er forms of the vowel-changing verbs.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Er ___ sehr schnell.
questions.explanation: In the er/sie/es form the stem vowel becomes i and the ending is t, so sprechen gives spricht.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Du ___ ein interessantes Buch.
questions.explanation: lesen changes e to ie in the du form; the stem already ends in s, so the du ending is just t: liest.

questions.prompt: Complete the reply: Ich fahre mit dem Bus. Und du? Du ___ auch den Bus.
questions.explanation: nehmen changes to nimm in the du form and takes the ending st, giving nimmst.

questions.prompt: Complete the question about your brother: Was ___ er gern, Pizza oder Salat?
questions.explanation: essen has the stem change e to i in the er form; the stem ends in ss, so the form is isst.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'She reads the newspaper every day.'
questions.explanation: The conjugated verb liest stays in position two, and the time expression jeden Tag comes before the accusative object die Zeitung.

activities.title: Beim Frühstück
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Over breakfast with a flatmate, the vowel-changing verbs keep coming up in questions and answers.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen! Was isst du zum Frühstück?
lines.en: Good morning! What do you eat for breakfast?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich esse ein Brötchen und ich nehme einen Kaffee.
lines.en: I eat a bread roll and I have a coffee.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Liest du die Zeitung am Morgen?
lines.en: Do you read the newspaper in the morning?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich lese die Zeitung, und mein Bruder sieht die Nachrichten.
lines.en: Yes, I read the newspaper, and my brother watches the news.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Spricht dein Bruder auch Deutsch?
lines.en: Does your brother speak German too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er spricht ein bisschen Deutsch, aber er spricht sehr langsam.
lines.en: He speaks a bit of German, but he speaks very slowly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nimmst du heute das Fahrrad?
lines.en: Are you taking the bike today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich nehme den Bus. Und du? Du nimmst doch das Fahrrad, oder?
lines.en: No, I am taking the bus. And you? You are taking the bike, right?

days.title: Die Uhrzeit, offiziell und im Alltag
days.objective: Ask for and tell the time in both the everyday style and the official 24 hour style.
days.paretoFocus: Trains, appointments and opening hours all run on the clock, and the everyday form halb acht trips up almost every beginner because it means 7:30 and not 8:30.
drills: Say the current time out loud every hour today, first in the everyday style, then in the official style.
Write six times such as 6:15, 7:30, 9:45 and say each one without looking at the rule.
Read three opening hours from any label or package in your home and say them as official times.

items: How do you ask a stranger what time it is?
What clock time does halb neun mean?
Say 16:45 in the official style and then in the everyday style.

activities.title: Wie spät ist es?
activities.intro: German has two clock systems. In everyday speech you count towards the next hour, so halb acht is 7:30, half way to eight. Official announcements and timetables use the 24 hour clock with the word Uhr between the hour and the minutes.

items.hr: Wie spät ist es? Es ist acht Uhr.
items.en: What time is it? It is eight o'clock.
items.note: Wie spät ist es? is the standard question

items.en: It is half past seven.
items.note: halb acht means 7:30, half way towards eight

items.en: It is a quarter past nine.
It is a quarter to ten.
The train leaves at 14:30.
items.note: official style: hour, the word Uhr, then the minutes

activities.title: Uhrzeit üben
activities.intro: Practise reading clock times in both German systems.

questions.prompt: It is 7:30. How do you say this in everyday German?
questions.explanation: Everyday German counts forward to the next full hour, so 7:30 is half way to eight: halb acht.

questions.prompt: It is 9:45. Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Fifteen minutes before the full hour is Viertel vor, and the hour named is the coming one, so 9:45 is Viertel vor zehn.

questions.prompt: It is 8:15. Complete: Es ist Viertel ___ acht.
questions.explanation: Minutes after the full hour take nach, so 8:15 is Viertel nach acht.

questions.prompt: Ask a stranger for the time: Wie ___ ist es?
questions.explanation: The fixed question is Wie spät ist es?, literally how late is it.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The train leaves at ten o'clock.'
questions.explanation: The conjugated verb fährt stays in position two, and a clock time is introduced by um.

activities.title: Am Bahnhof
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are at the station and you have to understand both the official times on the board and the everyday times a stranger uses.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Entschuldigung, wie spät ist es?
lines.en: Excuse me, what time is it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Es ist Viertel nach sieben.
lines.en: It is a quarter past seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Oh, gut. Wann fährt der Zug nach Köln?
lines.en: Oh, good. When does the train to Cologne leave?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der Zug fährt um 19 Uhr 30, also um halb acht.
lines.en: The train leaves at 19:30, so at half past seven.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann habe ich noch fünfzehn Minuten Zeit.
lines.en: Then I still have fifteen minutes.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber der nächste Zug fährt erst um Viertel vor neun.
lines.en: Yes, but the next train only leaves at a quarter to nine.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vielen Dank! Sie sind sehr nett.
lines.en: Thank you very much! You are very kind.

days.title: Wochentage, Monate und Jahreszeiten
days.objective: Name every weekday, month and season in German and use them to say when something happens.
days.paretoFocus: Every appointment, invitation and plan needs a day or a month, and these words are almost identical to English ones, so this is the fastest vocabulary win at this level.
drills: Say the seven weekdays out loud three times without stopping.
Name the twelve months and mark which season each one belongs to.
Write one true sentence about something you do in each of the four seasons.

items: Name the weekdays from Montag to Sonntag without looking.
Which German month is the ninth month, and how is it spelled?
Which two days make up das Wochenende?

activities.title: Tage, Monate und Jahreszeiten
activities.intro: All weekdays, months and seasons are masculine nouns and are always written with a capital letter. The only exception is das Wochenende. Many of them look very close to English, so listen for the German pronunciation rather than the spelling.

items.en: The week begins on Monday.
items.note: Montag, Dienstag, Mittwoch, Donnerstag, Freitag, Samstag, Sonntag

items.hr: Der Januar ist der erste Monat im Jahr.
items.en: January is the first month of the year.
items.note: in Germany the first month is Januar

items.en: At the weekend I am off work.
items.note: am Wochenende is a set phrase; das Wochenende is the only neuter word here

items.en: Spring begins in March.
items.note: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter

items.hr: Der Sommer ist warm, aber der Winter ist sehr kalt.
items.en: Summer is warm, but winter is very cold.
items.note: aber links two main clauses and does not change the word order

activities.title: Kalenderwörter
activities.intro: Practise the days, months and seasons of the German calendar.

questions.prompt: Which day comes directly after Dienstag?
questions.explanation: The order is Montag, Dienstag, Mittwoch, so the day after Dienstag is Mittwoch.

questions.prompt: Which season lies between Frühling and Herbst?
questions.explanation: The seasons run Frühling, Sommer, Herbst, Winter, so Sommer sits between spring and autumn.

questions.prompt: Name the twelfth month of the year: Der letzte Monat im Jahr heißt ___.
questions.explanation: The German months end with Oktober, November and Dezember, all written with a capital letter.

questions.prompt: Samstag und Sonntag zusammen sind das ___.
questions.explanation: das Wochenende is the neuter noun for the weekend and is capitalised like every German noun.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'In summer I have a lot of time.'
questions.explanation: When the time expression starts the sentence, the verb habe still holds position two and the subject ich follows it.

activities.title: Wann hast du Geburtstag?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks about your birthday and your favourite season, so you need days, months and seasons.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann hast du Geburtstag?
lines.en: When is your birthday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mein Geburtstag ist im Mai.
lines.en: My birthday is in May.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schön! Der Mai ist ein warmer Monat.
lines.en: Nice! May is a warm month.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, der Frühling ist meine Lieblingsjahreszeit.
lines.en: Yes, spring is my favourite season.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was machst du am Wochenende?
lines.en: And what are you doing at the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Samstag besuche ich meine Schwester, am Sonntag habe ich frei.
lines.en: On Saturday I am visiting my sister, on Sunday I am off.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und im Winter? Magst du den Schnee?
lines.en: And in winter? Do you like the snow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, der Winter ist mir zu kalt. Ich warte auf den Sommer.
lines.en: No, winter is too cold for me. I am waiting for summer.

days.title: Zeitangaben mit am, im und um
days.objective: Choose correctly between am, im and um when saying when something happens.
days.paretoFocus: Three tiny words cover almost all A1 time expressions, and picking the wrong one is the single most common beginner mistake when arranging a meeting.
drills: Write three sentences with am plus a weekday, three with im plus a month and three with um plus a clock time.
Say your whole week out loud using am for each day.
Take any five appointments from your own calendar and describe them in German using all three markers.

items: Which marker goes with a weekday, and which one goes with a month?
Which marker introduces a clock time?
Say 'On Friday at eight o'clock' in German.

activities.title: am, im, um
activities.intro: German uses three fixed markers for time. Use am with days and parts of the day, im with months and seasons, and um with clock times. Learn each combination as a whole chunk instead of translating the English preposition.

items.en: On Friday I have time.
items.note: am plus a weekday; learn am Montag, am Dienstag as set phrases

items.hr: Im August ist es sehr warm.
items.en: In August it is very warm.
items.note: im plus a month or a season

items.hr: Um sieben Uhr beginnt der Kurs.
items.en: The course starts at seven o'clock.
items.note: um plus a clock time

items.hr: Am Abend sehe ich gern einen Film.
items.en: In the evening I like watching a film.
items.note: am Morgen, am Nachmittag, am Abend; but in der Nacht

items.en: At the weekend I play football.
items.note: long vowel before ß in Fußball

activities.title: Wann genau?
activities.intro: Choose the right time marker for each expression.

questions.prompt: Choose the right marker: ___ Montag arbeite ich nicht.
questions.explanation: Weekdays always take am, so the sentence starts with Am Montag.

questions.prompt: Choose the right marker: Der Film beginnt ___ acht Uhr.
questions.explanation: Clock times are introduced by um, never by am or im.

questions.prompt: Complete with the marker for months: ___ Juli fahre ich nach Berlin.
questions.explanation: Months and seasons take im, and the word is capitalised here because it starts the sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete with the marker for clock times: Der Unterricht beginnt ___ neun Uhr.
questions.explanation: A stated hour on the clock always follows um.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'On Sunday I have no time.'
questions.explanation: The time expression takes position one, the verb habe stays in position two, and die Zeit is negated with keine.

activities.title: Ein Termin
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are fixing a meeting with a colleague and every line needs am, im or um.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du am Dienstag Zeit?
lines.en: Do you have time on Tuesday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Dienstag nicht, aber am Mittwoch habe ich Zeit.
lines.en: Not on Tuesday, but on Wednesday I have time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut. Um wie viel Uhr?
lines.en: Good. At what time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Um halb elf. Ist das gut für dich?
lines.en: At half past ten. Is that good for you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, perfekt. Und im September? Machst du Urlaub?
lines.en: Yes, perfect. And in September? Are you taking a holiday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Im September arbeite ich, aber im Oktober habe ich Urlaub.
lines.en: In September I work, but in October I have a holiday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann bis Mittwoch um halb elf!
lines.en: See you on Wednesday at half past ten then!

days.title: Das Modalverb können
days.objective: Use können in all present tense forms to talk about ability and possibility, with the second verb at the end.
days.paretoFocus: können is the first modal verb and it unlocks a whole sentence pattern: one conjugated verb early, one infinitive at the end, which is the backbone of German syntax.
drills: List five things you can do well and five you cannot do, each with können plus an infinitive.
Ask three questions with Kannst du that a friend could really answer.
Say the six forms of können out loud until they come without thinking.

items: What are the ich and er forms of können, and how do they differ from the infinitive?
Where does the second verb go in a sentence with a modal verb?
Say 'We can go to the cinema on Saturday' in German.

activities.title: Was kannst du?
activities.intro: können means can, to be able to. The singular forms lose the umlaut: ich kann, du kannst, er kann, while the plural keeps it: wir können, ihr könnt, sie können. Note that ich and er look identical and take no ending. The second verb always stands at the very end of the sentence in its plain infinitive form.

items.en: I can swim well.
items.note: ich kann has no ending

items.en: Can you speak German?
items.note: in a yes or no question the modal comes first, the infinitive still ends the sentence

items.hr: Er kann heute leider nicht kommen.
items.en: Unfortunately he cannot come today.
items.note: nicht stands before the infinitive at the end

items.hr: Wir können am Samstag ins Kino gehen.
items.en: We can go to the cinema on Saturday.
items.note: ins is in plus das, a contraction with the accusative

items.en: Can you hear me?
items.note: ihr könnt; mich is the accusative of ich

activities.title: können üben
activities.intro: Practise the forms of können and the position of the second verb.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: ___ du Auto fahren?
questions.explanation: The du form of können is kannst, without the umlaut and with the ending st.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct word order?
questions.explanation: The modal verb takes position two and the infinitive closes the sentence, so everything else sits between them.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence about your father: Er ___ sehr gut kochen.
questions.explanation: The er form of können is kann, identical to the ich form and with no ending.

questions.prompt: Complete the polite refusal: Wir ___ heute leider nicht kommen.
questions.explanation: The wir form keeps the umlaut and takes the ending en: wir können.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Can you speak German?' (informal, one person)
questions.explanation: A yes or no question starts with the conjugated modal, the subject follows, and the infinitive stays at the end.

activities.title: Kannst du das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A new classmate asks what you are able to do, so every answer uses können plus an infinitive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kannst du kochen?
lines.en: Can you cook?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich kann gut kochen. Ich koche jeden Tag.
lines.en: Yes, I can cook well. I cook every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und kannst du auch Gitarre spielen?
lines.en: And can you play the guitar too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, das kann ich nicht. Aber meine Schwester kann Klavier spielen.
lines.en: No, I cannot do that. But my sister can play the piano.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Interessant! Können wir am Freitag zusammen lernen?
lines.en: Interesting! Can we study together on Friday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Freitag kann ich leider nicht, aber am Samstag geht es.
lines.en: On Friday I unfortunately cannot, but Saturday works.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut, dann können wir am Samstag um drei Uhr anfangen.
lines.en: Good, then we can start on Saturday at three o'clock.

days.title: Das Modalverb müssen
days.objective: Use müssen to express obligation and necessity, keeping the infinitive at the end of the sentence.
days.paretoFocus: Half of everyday small talk is about what you have to do, and müssen is also the verb you need to explain politely why you have to leave.
drills: Write five things you have to do this week with müssen plus an infinitive.
Turn three of those sentences into questions with Musst du.
Say the six forms of müssen out loud and notice which ones lose the umlaut.

items: Which forms of müssen have no umlaut?
Say 'I have to work today' and 'You have to take the bus' in German.
Which set phrases did this lesson use for going to the doctor and going home?

activities.title: Was musst du machen?
activities.intro: müssen means must, to have to. Like können it loses the umlaut in the singular: ich muss, du musst, er muss, and keeps it in the plural: wir müssen, ihr müsst, sie müssen. The ich and er forms are again identical and take no ending. Watch the spelling: a short u before the double s.

items.en: I have to work for a long time today.
items.note: short u, so muss with ss

items.en: You have to take the bus.
items.note: den Bus is the accusative object of nehmen

items.hr: Er muss morgen zum Arzt gehen.
items.en: He has to go to the doctor tomorrow.
items.note: zum Arzt is a set phrase, learn it whole

items.hr: Wir müssen noch Brot und Milch kaufen.
items.en: We still have to buy bread and milk.
items.note: wir müssen keeps the umlaut

items.hr: Ich muss jetzt nach Hause gehen.
items.en: I have to go home now.
items.note: nach Hause is a set phrase for going home

activities.title: müssen üben
activities.intro: Practise the forms of müssen and where the second verb belongs.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Ich ___ heute früh anfangen.
questions.explanation: The ich form of müssen is muss, with no ending and no umlaut.

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the infinitive correctly?
questions.explanation: The infinitive kaufen must be the last word, so the object Brot comes before it.

questions.prompt: Give a friend health advice: Du ___ mehr Wasser trinken.
questions.explanation: The du form takes st on the stem muss, giving musst.

questions.prompt: Tell a group of guests about the bill: Ihr ___ die Rechnung bezahlen.
questions.explanation: The ihr form keeps the umlaut and adds t: ihr müsst.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have to go home now.'
questions.explanation: muss holds position two, the set phrase nach Hause sits inside the sentence, and gehen closes it.

activities.title: Keine Zeit heute
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend invites you out and you explain with müssen why you are busy.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kommst du heute Abend mit ins Kino?
lines.en: Are you coming to the cinema with us this evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Leider nicht, ich muss heute lange arbeiten.
lines.en: Unfortunately not, I have to work for a long time today.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade. Und morgen?
lines.en: What a pity. And tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Morgen muss ich zum Arzt gehen, aber am Freitag habe ich Zeit.
lines.en: Tomorrow I have to go to the doctor, but on Friday I have time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut. Musst du am Freitag auch arbeiten?
lines.en: Good. Do you have to work on Friday too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, aber ich muss noch Brot und Milch kaufen.
lines.en: No, but I still have to buy bread and milk.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kein Problem, wir müssen erst um acht Uhr da sein.
lines.en: No problem, we only have to be there at eight o'clock.

days.title: Die Modalverben wollen und möchten
days.objective: Express what you want with wollen and make the same wish politely with möchten.
days.paretoFocus: Germans hear ich will as blunt and ich möchte as polite, so choosing between the two decides whether you sound demanding or friendly in shops, cafes and offices.
drills: Write five plans for the weekend with wollen plus an infinitive.
Rewrite three of them as polite wishes with möchten.
Say what three family members want to do this year.

items: Which of the two verbs is the polite one when you order something?
What are the ich and du forms of möchten?
Say 'We want to go to Hamburg at the weekend' in German.

activities.title: wollen und möchten
activities.intro: wollen states a plain wish or intention: ich will, du willst, er will, wir wollen, ihr wollt, sie wollen. möchten is the polite version and is the form you use in shops and restaurants: ich möchte, du möchtest, er möchte, wir möchten, ihr möchtet, sie möchten. Both keep the infinitive at the end, and möchten can also stand alone with just an object.

items.hr: Ich will heute Abend einen Film sehen.
items.en: I want to watch a film this evening.
items.note: ich will has no ending

items.en: Would you like a coffee?
items.note: möchten can stand without a second verb

items.hr: Wir wollen am Wochenende nach Hamburg fahren.
items.en: We want to travel to Hamburg at the weekend.
items.note: nach plus a city name means to that city

items.hr: Er möchte einen Tisch für zwei Personen.
items.en: He would like a table for two people.
items.note: für always takes the accusative: für zwei Personen

items.hr: Was willst du am Samstag machen?
items.en: What do you want to do on Saturday?
items.note: du willst; the question word takes position one and the modal position two

activities.title: Wunsch und Höflichkeit
activities.intro: Practise choosing between the direct and the polite way of expressing a wish.

questions.prompt: Which form is the polite one when you order in a restaurant?
questions.explanation: ich möchte is the polite wish form; ich will sounds blunt and demanding when ordering.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Wir ___ heute ins Kino gehen.
questions.explanation: The wir form of wollen is wollen, identical to the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Offer a friend a tea politely: ___ du einen Tee?
questions.explanation: The du form of the polite wish verb is möchtest, and it is capitalised here as the first word.

questions.prompt: Say what your brother intends to buy: Er ___ ein neues Fahrrad kaufen.
questions.explanation: The er form of wollen is will, with no ending, exactly like the ich form.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I would like a coffee, please.'
questions.explanation: möchte stands in position two and der Kaffee becomes einen Kaffee as the accusative object.

activities.title: Pläne für das Wochenende
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are making weekend plans, switching between the direct wollen and the polite möchten.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was willst du am Samstag machen?
lines.en: What do you want to do on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich will nach Hamburg fahren. Willst du mitkommen?
lines.en: I want to travel to Hamburg. Do you want to come along?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gern! Wollen wir den Zug um acht Uhr nehmen?
lines.en: Gladly! Shall we take the eight o'clock train?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber vorher möchte ich einen Kaffee trinken.
lines.en: Yes, but before that I would like to drink a coffee.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kein Problem. Möchtest du auch ein Brötchen?
lines.en: No problem. Would you like a bread roll too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, bitte. Und am Abend wollen wir ein Konzert sehen, oder?
lines.en: Yes, please. And in the evening we want to see a concert, right?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Genau. Mein Bruder möchte auch mitkommen.
