lines.en: Exactly. My brother would like to come along too.

days.title: Die Modalverben dürfen und sollen
days.objective: Use dürfen for permission and prohibition and sollen for advice or an instruction from someone else.
days.paretoFocus: Signs, house rules and doctors all speak in dürfen and sollen, so these two verbs let you read public notices and understand what is expected of you.
drills: Write three rules of your workplace or home using dürfen and nicht.
Write three pieces of advice a doctor could give you using sollen.
Ask for permission three times with Darf ich in different situations.

items: Which verb do you use to ask for permission?
Which verb reports advice or an order that comes from another person?
Say 'Smoking is not allowed here' in German using man.

activities.title: dürfen und sollen
activities.intro: dürfen means to be allowed to: ich darf, du darfst, er darf, wir dürfen, ihr dürft, sie dürfen. With nicht it becomes a prohibition. sollen reports an instruction or advice from someone else and keeps its vowel throughout: ich soll, du sollst, er soll, wir sollen, ihr sollt, sie sollen. Both put the second verb at the end.

items.en: Smoking is not allowed here.
items.note: man means people in general and always takes the er form

items.en: May I park here?
items.note: Darf ich is the standard way to ask permission

items.en: You should drink more water.
items.note: sollen reports advice from someone else, for example a doctor

items.hr: Kinder dürfen den Film nicht sehen.
items.en: Children are not allowed to watch the film.
items.note: nicht comes before the infinitive at the end

items.en: What should I cook today?
items.note: ich soll has no ending

activities.title: Erlaubnis und Rat
activities.intro: Practise the difference between permission and advice.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: ___ ich das Fenster öffnen?
questions.explanation: The ich form of dürfen is darf, with no ending and no umlaut.

questions.prompt: Your doctor gives you advice, not a rule. Which sentence fits?
questions.explanation: sollen reports what another person tells you to do, so it is the verb for advice or instructions.

questions.prompt: Read the sign aloud: Hier ___ man nicht rauchen.
questions.explanation: man takes the er form, and dürfen with nicht expresses a prohibition: hier darf man nicht.

questions.prompt: Ask your partner for cooking instructions: Was ___ ich kochen, Fisch oder Fleisch?
questions.explanation: You are asking what someone else wants you to do, so you use sollen; the ich form is soll.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'You are not allowed to park here.' (informal)
questions.explanation: darfst holds position two, nicht stands directly before the infinitive, and parken ends the sentence.

activities.title: Die Regeln im Museum
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are a visitor asking a museum attendant what is allowed and what you are supposed to do.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag! Kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.en: Good afternoon! Can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, darf ich hier fotografieren?
lines.en: Yes, may I take photos here?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fotografieren dürfen Sie, aber ohne Blitz.
lines.en: You may take photos, but without flash.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut. Und darf man hier essen und trinken?
lines.en: Good. And is eating and drinking allowed here?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nein, hier darf man nicht essen. Sie sollen die Tasche unten lassen.
lines.en: No, eating is not allowed here. You are supposed to leave your bag downstairs.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Verstehe. Wo soll ich die Tasche lassen?
lines.en: I see. Where should I leave the bag?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dort links. Und Kinder dürfen die Bilder nicht anfassen.
lines.en: Over there on the left. And children are not allowed to touch the pictures.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alles klar, vielen Dank!
lines.en: All clear, thank you very much!

days.title: Die Satzklammer, wohin das zweite Verb gehört
days.objective: Apply the sentence bracket: keep the conjugated verb in position two and push the infinitive, the separable prefix or the participle to the very end.
days.paretoFocus: This one rule explains almost every German word order that feels strange to English speakers, and once you hear the bracket you can build long sentences without ever losing the verb.
drills: Take five short sentences you already know and expand each one with a modal verb, moving the main verb to the end.
Underline the two ends of the bracket in ten German sentences you find or write.
Say three sentences that start with a time expression and check that the conjugated verb still comes second.

items: Which position does the conjugated verb hold in a statement?
Name the three things that can close the bracket at the end of the sentence.
Where does the conjugated verb stand in a yes or no question?

activities.title: Die Satzklammer
activities.intro: German sentences are built like a bracket. The conjugated verb holds position two in a statement, and the second verb part waits at the very end. That end part can be an infinitive after a modal, a separable prefix, or later a past participle. Everything else, objects, times and places, sits inside the bracket between the two verb parts.

items.hr: Ich kann heute Abend nicht kommen.
items.en: I cannot come this evening.
items.note: kann opens the bracket in position two, kommen closes it at the end

items.hr: Wir wollen am Samstag einen Film sehen.
items.en: We want to watch a film on Saturday.
items.note: the time and the object sit inside the bracket

items.hr: Willst du morgen einen Kuchen backen?
items.en: Do you want to bake a cake tomorrow?
items.note: in a yes or no question the conjugated verb moves to position one, but the infinitive still ends the sentence

items.en: I am closing the window.
items.note: with a separable verb the prefix zu takes the end position of the bracket

items.hr: Am Montag muss er um acht Uhr im Büro sein.
items.en: On Monday he has to be in the office at eight o'clock.
items.note: im Büro is a set phrase; the time expression takes position one so the verb stays second

activities.title: Klammer auf, Klammer zu
activities.intro: Practise putting each verb part in its correct position.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct bracket?
questions.explanation: The conjugated verb will is second and the infinitive trinken is last, with the object inside the bracket.

questions.prompt: In a German statement, where does the infinitive stand?
options: at the end of the sentence
questions.answer: at the end of the sentence
questions.explanation: The infinitive closes the bracket, so it is always the last element of the statement.

questions.prompt: Close the bracket with the missing infinitive: Wir müssen heute noch Brot ___.
questions.explanation: After a modal verb the second verb stays in its plain infinitive form and takes the final position.

questions.prompt: Put the right conjugated verb in position two: Am Montag ___ ich früh arbeiten.
questions.explanation: When a time expression opens the sentence, the conjugated verb still holds position two, so muss comes before the subject ich.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'On Saturday we want to watch a film.'
questions.explanation: The time expression takes position one, wollen holds position two, the subject follows, and sehen closes the bracket.

activities.title: Was machen wir heute Abend?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend plan the evening, and every sentence shows the bracket with a modal verb and a final infinitive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was wollen wir heute Abend machen?
lines.en: What do we want to do this evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich möchte einen Film sehen. Kannst du um acht Uhr kommen?
lines.en: I would like to watch a film. Can you come at eight o'clock?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Um acht kann ich nicht, ich muss bis sieben Uhr arbeiten.
lines.en: At eight I cannot, I have to work until seven o'clock.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann können wir den Film um halb neun anfangen.
lines.en: Then we can start the film at half past eight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut. Soll ich Brot und Käse mitbringen?
lines.en: Good. Should I bring bread and cheese?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, bitte. Ich will noch schnell Wasser kaufen.
lines.en: Yes, please. I still want to quickly buy water.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfekt, dann muss ich jetzt nach Hause gehen.
lines.en: Perfect, then I have to go home now.

days.title: Trennbare Verben
days.objective: Split separable verbs correctly in a main clause and keep them whole after a modal verb.
days.paretoFocus: Separable verbs cover the entire daily routine, getting up, starting, calling, shopping, and beginners lose the meaning of a whole sentence when they miss the little prefix at the end.
drills: Write five sentences about your morning using aufstehen, anfangen, einkaufen and anrufen.
Take three of those sentences and add a modal verb, then check that the prefix rejoins the verb.
Listen to any German sentence you know and say what the last word tells you about the verb.

items: Where does the prefix of a separable verb go in a normal statement?
What happens to the prefix after a modal verb?
Say 'I get up at seven o'clock' and 'I have to get up at seven o'clock' in German.

activities.title: Der Vorsilbe hinterher
activities.intro: Many German verbs carry a stressed prefix such as auf, an, ein or mit. In a main clause the verb is conjugated normally and the prefix jumps to the end of the sentence, closing the bracket you learned in the previous lesson. After a modal verb there is no bracket to fill, so the verb stays in one piece as an infinitive.

items.hr: Ich stehe um sechs Uhr auf.
items.en: I get up at six o'clock.
items.note: aufstehen: ich stehe ... auf

items.hr: Der Kurs fängt um neun Uhr an.
items.en: The course starts at nine o'clock.
items.note: anfangen: er fängt ... an, with the a to ä change in the stem

items.en: When do you do the shopping?
items.note: einkaufen: the prefix ein still goes last, even in a question

items.hr: Ich rufe dich am Abend an.
items.en: I will call you in the evening.
items.note: anrufen; dich is the accusative of du

items.en: I have to get up early tomorrow.
items.note: after a modal the verb stays whole as an infinitive at the end

activities.title: Verb und Vorsilbe
activities.intro: Practise splitting and rejoining separable verbs.

questions.prompt: Which sentence splits the separable verb correctly?
options: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
Ich stehe auf um sieben Uhr.
Um sieben Uhr ich stehe auf.
questions.answer: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
questions.explanation: The conjugated part stays in position two and the prefix auf goes to the very end of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete correctly: Ich muss morgen früh ___.
questions.explanation: After a modal verb the separable verb is used as one plain infinitive, so the prefix stays attached.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence about the film: Der Film ___ um acht Uhr an.
questions.explanation: anfangen has the stem change a to ä in the er form, and the prefix an already sits at the end.

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing prefix: Am Sonntag stehe ich erst um zehn Uhr ___.
questions.explanation: The verb is aufstehen, so the prefix always takes the final position in a main clause.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I do the shopping on Saturday.'
questions.explanation: einkaufen splits: kaufe stays in position two and the prefix ein closes the sentence.

activities.title: Wann fängt der Kurs an?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A classmate asks about your schedule, and every answer uses a separable verb.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann stehst du am Morgen auf?
lines.en: When do you get up in the morning?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich stehe um halb sieben auf.
lines.en: I get up at half past six.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: So früh! Wann fängt dein Kurs an?
lines.en: So early! When does your course start?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Der Kurs fängt um neun Uhr an und endet um eins.
lines.en: The course starts at nine o'clock and ends at one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wann kaufst du ein?
lines.en: And when do you do your shopping?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich kaufe am Freitag ein, aber heute muss ich früh aufstehen.
lines.en: I do the shopping on Friday, but today I have to get up early.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut. Rufst du mich am Abend an?
lines.en: Good. Will you call me in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich rufe dich um acht Uhr an.
lines.en: Yes, I will call you at eight o'clock.

days.title: Der Imperativ mit du, ihr und Sie
days.objective: Give instructions and requests in all three German command forms.
days.paretoFocus: Instructions are everywhere, in recipes, in class and on signs, and using the wrong command form is what makes a request sound rude rather than friendly.
drills: Turn five du sentences you know into commands by dropping du and the ending.
Give the same three instructions to one friend, to a group of friends and to a stranger.
Write a short recipe of four steps using only imperative forms.

items: How do you form the du command, and which word disappears?
Which command form still contains the pronoun?
Give the du command of sprechen and explain the vowel.

activities.title: Sag es freundlich
activities.intro: German has three command forms. For du you take the present tense stem and drop both the pronoun and the ending. For ihr you use the normal present tense form without the pronoun. For Sie you keep the pronoun after the verb. The word bitte makes any of them polite.

items.en: Please come at eight o'clock!
items.note: du form: the stem alone, without du and without the ending st

items.en: Please come on time!
items.note: ihr form: the normal present tense form without ihr

items.en: Please take a seat!
items.note: Sie form: verb first, then Sie; Platz nehmen is a set phrase

items.en: Please speak slowly!
items.note: verbs with e to i keep the change in the du command: du sprichst gives sprich

items.en: Please open the window!
items.note: aufmachen splits, so the prefix auf still ends the sentence

activities.title: Bitte mach das!
activities.intro: Practise building commands for one person, for a group and for a stranger.

questions.prompt: What is the du command of sprechen?
questions.explanation: Verbs with the e to i change keep the new vowel in the du command and drop the ending, giving sprich.

questions.prompt: What is the Sie command of kommen?
questions.explanation: The polite command puts the infinitive form first and keeps the pronoun Sie directly after it.

questions.prompt: Tell one friend to take the bus, using nehmen in the du command form: ___ bitte den Bus!
questions.explanation: Verbs with the e to i change keep the new vowel in the du command and drop the ending, so nehmen gives Nimm.

questions.prompt: Tell a group of friends to wait here, using the verb warten: ___ bitte hier!
questions.explanation: The ihr command is the normal ihr form without the pronoun, and warten needs an extra e before the t: wartet.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Please open the window.' (informal, one person)
questions.explanation: The du command opens the sentence, bitte softens it, and the separable prefix auf closes the bracket.

activities.title: In der Küche
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are cooking with a friend and you give and receive short instructions in the imperative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich möchte kochen. Sag mir bitte, was ich machen soll.
lines.en: I would like to cook. Please tell me what I should do.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nimm bitte das Messer und schneide die Tomaten!
lines.en: Please take the knife and cut the tomatoes!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dann? Wie viel Wasser brauchen wir?
lines.en: And then? How much water do we need?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mach das Wasser heiß und warte fünf Minuten!
lines.en: Heat the water and wait five minutes!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Meine Eltern kommen auch. Was sage ich ihnen?
lines.en: My parents are coming too. What do I say to them?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sag: Kommt bitte um sieben Uhr! Und zu deinem Chef: Kommen Sie bitte auch!
lines.en: Say: Please come at seven o'clock! And to your boss: Please come as well!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gut. Und das Fenster?
lines.en: Good. And the window?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mach bitte das Fenster auf, hier ist es sehr warm.
lines.en: Please open the window, it is very warm in here.

days.title: Im Restaurant bestellen
days.objective: Order food and drink in a restaurant, ask for a recommendation and pay the bill.
days.paretoFocus: Ordering a meal is the first real conversation most learners have in Germany, and it reuses möchten, the accusative and prices in one short exchange.
drills: Order a starter, a main dish and a drink out loud with ich möchte and ich nehme.
Practise saying five prices in the German style with a comma.
Rehearse asking for the bill and answering the question zusammen oder getrennt.

items: How do you ask a waiter for a recommendation?
How do you write and say four euros fifty in German?
What are the two ways a group can pay, and how does the waiter ask?

activities.title: Bestellen und bezahlen
activities.intro: In a restaurant you order with ich möchte or ich nehme, both followed by an accusative object. Prices are written with a comma, so 4,50 Euro is spoken as vier Euro fünfzig. When you pay, the waiter usually asks whether the group pays together or separately.

items.en: I would like the menu, please.
items.note: die Speisekarte is the menu

items.en: What can you recommend?
items.note: empfehlen has the e to ie change: Sie empfehlen, er empfiehlt

items.hr: Ich nehme die Suppe und ein Glas Wasser.
items.en: I will have the soup and a glass of water.
items.note: ich nehme is the normal way to order a dish

items.en: The soup costs four euros fifty.
items.note: German prices use a comma, not a full stop

items.en: The bill, please! Together or separately?
items.note: Zahlen bitte is the standard way to ask for the bill

activities.title: Im Lokal
activities.intro: Practise the standard phrases you need at a restaurant table.

questions.prompt: You want to order the soup. What do you say?
questions.explanation: ich nehme plus an accusative object is the standard ordering phrase; the other verbs describe cooking or selling.

questions.prompt: How is a price written in German?
questions.explanation: German uses a comma as the decimal separator, so four euros fifty is written 4,50 Euro.

questions.prompt: Ask the waiter for the menu: Ich möchte bitte die ___.
questions.explanation: die Speisekarte is the list of dishes, and every German noun keeps its capital letter.

questions.prompt: You want the bill. Call the waiter with the standard word plus bitte: ___ bitte!
questions.explanation: Zahlen bitte is the fixed phrase for asking to pay, and it is capitalised as the first word.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I would like a glass of water, please.'
questions.explanation: möchte holds position two and das Glas stays ein Glas in the accusative because it is neuter.

activities.title: Ein Tisch für zwei
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are in a restaurant with a friend and you order, ask about the dish of the day and pay.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Abend! Haben Sie reserviert?
lines.en: Good evening! Do you have a reservation?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, aber ich möchte einen Tisch für zwei Personen.
lines.en: No, but I would like a table for two people.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gern, hier bitte. Möchten Sie die Speisekarte?
lines.en: Gladly, here you are. Would you like the menu?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, bitte. Was können Sie heute empfehlen?
lines.en: Yes, please. What can you recommend today?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Die Suppe ist sehr gut, sie kostet 4,50 Euro.
lines.en: The soup is very good, it costs four euros fifty.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, ich nehme die Suppe und ein Glas Wasser.
lines.en: Good, I will have the soup and a glass of water.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sehr gern. Möchten Sie später zahlen, zusammen oder getrennt?
lines.en: With pleasure. Would you like to pay later, together or separately?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Getrennt bitte. Und ich möchte auch einen Kaffee.
lines.en: Separately, please. And I would also like a coffee.

days.title: Einkaufen, Mengen und Verpackungen
days.objective: Ask for exact quantities and packaging units when buying food.
days.paretoFocus: German shops sell by weight and by container, so without ein Kilo, 200 Gramm, eine Flasche and eine Packung you cannot buy anything precisely.
drills: Write a shopping list of eight items, each with a quantity or a container word.
Say the weights 100, 200 and 500 Gramm and half a kilo out loud.
Match five foods in your kitchen to the container word a German shop would use.

items: Which container word goes with Milch and which one with Butter?
How do you ask for two hundred grams of cheese?
What is the standard German word for a shopping bag?

activities.title: Mengen und Verpackungen
activities.intro: German counts food by weight in Gramm and Kilo and by container words such as Flasche, Packung, Becher and Stück. The container word comes first and the food follows directly with no article and no ending, for example ein Kilo Kartoffeln.

items.en: I am buying a kilo of potatoes.
items.note: das Kilo; the food follows with no extra word

items.en: Two hundred grams of cheese, please.
items.note: das Gramm stays singular after a number

items.hr: Ich brauche eine Flasche Milch und eine Packung Butter.
items.en: I need a bottle of milk and a packet of butter.
items.note: die Flasche, die Packung

items.hr: Wir brauchen noch einen Becher Sahne.
items.en: We still need a tub of cream.
items.note: der Becher; die Sahne is the standard word for cream

items.en: Please take a bag.
items.note: die Tüte is the standard German word for a shopping bag

activities.title: Wie viel darf es sein?
activities.intro: Practise quantities, weights and the right container word.

questions.prompt: Which quantity is the smallest?
questions.explanation: One kilo is one thousand grams, so 200 Gramm is far less than half a kilo, which is 500 Gramm.

questions.prompt: Which container word goes with Milch?
questions.explanation: Liquids are sold in bottles, so die Milch takes eine Flasche; Kilo is for weight and Stück for single pieces.

questions.prompt: Ask for two hundred grams of cheese: 200 Gramm ___, bitte.
questions.explanation: The food follows the weight directly with no article, and der Käse keeps its capital letter.

questions.prompt: Say you need one thousand grams of potatoes: Ich brauche ein ___ Kartoffeln.
questions.explanation: One thousand grams is ein Kilo, and the food follows immediately without an article.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I need a packet of butter.'
questions.explanation: brauche holds position two and die Packung is feminine, so the accusative article stays eine.

activities.title: Auf dem Markt
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are buying food at a market stall and every request needs a quantity or a container word.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen! Was darf es sein?
lines.en: Good morning! What can I get you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich möchte ein Kilo Kartoffeln, bitte.
lines.en: I would like a kilo of potatoes, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gern. Sonst noch etwas?
lines.en: Gladly. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, 200 Gramm Käse und eine Flasche Milch.
lines.en: Yes, two hundred grams of cheese and a bottle of milk.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Der Käse ist heute sehr gut. Brauchen Sie auch Butter?
lines.en: The cheese is very good today. Do you need butter as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, eine Packung Butter und einen Becher Sahne.
lines.en: Yes, a packet of butter and a tub of cream.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Das macht 12,40 Euro. Möchten Sie eine Tüte?
lines.en: That comes to twelve euros forty. Would you like a bag?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein danke, ich habe eine Tasche.
lines.en: No thank you, I have a bag with me.

days.title: Die Wohnung und die Möbel
days.objective: You can name the rooms of a flat and the main pieces of furniture, always with the correct article.
days.paretoFocus: Housing words come up the moment you visit someone, rent a room or answer the question where you live, and they carry the gender you will need for every later case.
drills: Walk through your home and say the German name of each room out loud with its article.
Name five objects you can see right now and put the correct article in front of each one.
Describe your flat in four sentences using es gibt and the accusative.

items: Which three rooms of a home can you name with their articles?
How do you say 'There is a sofa and an armchair' in German?
Which article goes with Bad, and which one goes with Küche?

activities.title: Meine Wohnung
activities.intro: German homes are described room by room, and every room and every piece of furniture carries a gender you should learn together with the word. Use es gibt plus the accusative to say what a room contains. An adjective like klein or neu stands after the verb sein, never in front of the noun.

items.hr: Das ist meine Wohnung. Sie hat drei Zimmer.
items.en: This is my flat. It has three rooms.
items.note: die Wohnung, das Zimmer

items.hr: Das Wohnzimmer ist groß. Es gibt ein Sofa und einen Sessel.
items.en: The living room is big. There is a sofa and an armchair.
items.note: es gibt + Akkusativ: einen Sessel

items.hr: Mein Schlafzimmer hat ein Bett, einen Schrank und eine Lampe.
items.en: My bedroom has a bed, a wardrobe and a lamp.
items.note: das Bett, der Schrank, die Lampe

items.hr: Das Bad ist klein, aber die Dusche ist neu.
items.en: The bathroom is small, but the shower is new.
items.note: adjective after sein, no ending

items.hr: Wir haben auch einen Balkon. Der Balkon ist mein Lieblingsplatz.
items.en: We also have a balcony. The balcony is my favourite spot.
items.note: der Balkon -> einen Balkon im Akkusativ

activities.title: Zimmer und Möbel üben
activities.intro: Practise the articles of rooms and furniture and the accusative after haben and es gibt.

questions.prompt: Which article belongs to the word Küche?
questions.explanation: Küche is feminine, so the article is die. Learn the article together with the noun.

questions.prompt: Wir haben ___ Balkon.
questions.explanation: Balkon is masculine and it is the direct object of haben, so the masculine accusative form einen is used.

questions.prompt: Complete with the definite article: '___ Bad ist klein.'
questions.explanation: Bad is neuter, so the definite article is das. It is the subject here, so it stays in the nominative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the indefinite article in the accusative: 'Ich brauche ___ Schrank.'
questions.explanation: Schrank is masculine and stands as the object of brauchen, so ein becomes einen.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The living room is big.'
questions.explanation: The verb ist stands in second position and the adjective groß follows it without any ending.

activities.title: Die neue Wohnung
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Show a friend around your new flat and describe the rooms and the furniture with the right articles.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie ist deine neue Wohnung?
lines.en: What is your new flat like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie ist schön. Sie hat drei Zimmer und einen Balkon.
lines.en: It is nice. It has three rooms and a balcony.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du schon Möbel?
lines.en: Do you have furniture already?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe ein Bett, einen Tisch und zwei Stühle.
lines.en: Yes, I have a bed, a table and two chairs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wie ist die Küche?
lines.en: And what is the kitchen like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Die Küche ist klein, aber der Kühlschrank ist neu.
lines.en: The kitchen is small, but the fridge is new.

days.title: Wo etwas ist, Präpositionen mit Dativ
days.objective: You can say where a thing or a person is, using in, an, auf, unter, über, neben, zwischen, vor and hinter with the dative.
days.paretoFocus: Saying where something is turns your vocabulary into real sentences, and this is also your first contact with the dative, the case you will meet every day from now on.
drills: Say where five objects in your room are, each time with a different preposition.
Write the dative form of der, das, die and the plural die on a card and test yourself.
Answer the question 'Wo ist das?' about ten things around you, out loud.

items: What happens to der, das, die and the plural die in the dative?
Which two contractions replace in dem and an dem?
How do you say 'The book is lying under the bed'?

activities.title: Wo ist das?
activities.intro: To answer the question wo, nine prepositions take the dative: unter, über, neben, in, an, auf, zwischen, vor and hinter. In the dative der and das both become dem, die becomes der, and the plural die becomes den with an extra n on the noun. These same prepositions have a second use with another case, but that comes later, so for now treat them simply as location words.

items.hr: Die Lampe steht auf dem Tisch.
items.en: The lamp is standing on the table.
items.note: der Tisch -> auf dem Tisch

items.hr: Das Buch liegt unter dem Bett.
items.en: The book is lying under the bed.
items.note: das Bett -> unter dem Bett

items.hr: Die Milch ist in der Küche. Ich bin im Bad.
items.en: The milk is in the kitchen. I am in the bathroom.
items.note: die Küche -> in der Küche; in dem = im

items.hr: Das Bild hängt an der Wand, neben dem Fenster.
items.en: The picture is hanging on the wall, next to the window.
items.note: die Wand -> an der Wand; das Fenster -> neben dem Fenster

items.hr: Die Katze schläft zwischen den Kissen.
items.en: The cat is sleeping between the cushions.
items.note: Plural: die Kissen -> zwischen den Kissen, plus an extra n

activities.title: Der Dativ nach Präpositionen
activities.intro: Practise the dative article after prepositions of place.

questions.prompt: Das Bild hängt an ___ Wand.
questions.explanation: Wand is feminine, and in the dative die becomes der.

questions.prompt: Der Ball liegt unter ___ Sofa.
questions.explanation: Sofa is neuter, and in the dative das becomes dem.

questions.prompt: Complete with the short form of the two words: 'Wo ist die Butter? Sie ist ___ Kühlschrank.'
questions.explanation: Kühlschrank is masculine, so in dem is required, and in dem is normally shortened to im.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative article: 'Die Schuhe stehen vor ___ Tür.'
questions.explanation: Tür is feminine, so the dative article after vor is der.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The lamp is standing on the table.'
questions.explanation: The verb steht stands second, and auf is followed by the dative dem Tisch because the sentence answers wo.

activities.title: Wo sind meine Schlüssel?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your flatmate is looking for the keys, so suggest places using prepositions with the dative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo sind meine Schlüssel?
lines.en: Where are my keys?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielleicht liegen sie auf dem Tisch.
lines.en: Maybe they are lying on the table.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nein, da sind sie nicht.
lines.en: No, they are not there.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dann sind sie in der Tasche.
lines.en: Then they are in the bag.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Auch nicht. Und im Bad?
lines.en: Not there either. And in the bathroom?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich sehe sie! Sie liegen unter der Zeitung.
lines.en: I can see them! They are lying under the newspaper.

days.title: Den Weg beschreiben
days.objective: You can ask how to get somewhere and give a simple route with geradeaus, links, rechts and the preposition zu.
days.paretoFocus: Asking for and understanding a route is one of the few situations where you must react to real speech straight away, and the polite imperative you already know does all the work.
drills: Describe out loud the route from your home to the nearest shop, in German.
Ask three different wie komme ich questions with zum and zur.
Say where four places in your town are, using neben, zwischen and vor.

items: What do zu dem and zu der become in normal speech?
How do you tell someone politely to go straight ahead?
How do you say 'The pharmacy is between the bank and the post office'?

activities.title: Wie komme ich dahin?
activities.intro: To ask for a route you say wie komme ich plus zu and a place, and zu always takes the dative. Zu dem is shortened to zum and zu der to zur, and you will almost never hear the long forms. The answer usually comes as a polite imperative with Sie.

items.hr: Entschuldigung, wie komme ich zum Bahnhof?
items.en: Excuse me, how do I get to the station?
items.note: der Bahnhof, zu dem = zum

items.hr: Gehen Sie hier geradeaus und dann links.
items.en: Go straight ahead here and then left.
items.note: polite imperative with Sie

items.hr: An der Ampel gehen Sie nach rechts.
items.en: At the traffic lights you go right.
items.note: die Ampel -> an der Ampel

items.hr: Die Apotheke ist zwischen der Bank und der Post.
items.en: The pharmacy is between the bank and the post office.
items.note: zwischen + Dativ twice

items.hr: Wie komme ich zur Post? Das ist nicht weit.
items.en: How do I get to the post office? That is not far.
items.note: die Post, zu der = zur

activities.title: Der Weg in der Stadt
activities.intro: Practise route questions with zum and zur and the words for directions.

questions.prompt: Wie komme ich ___ Bahnhof?
questions.explanation: Bahnhof is masculine, so zu dem is required, and zu dem is shortened to zum.

questions.prompt: You want to send someone to the right at the traffic lights. Which sentence do you say?
options: Gehen Sie an der Ampel nach rechts.
Gehen Sie an der Ampel nach links.
Gehen Sie an der Ampel geradeaus.
Gehen Sie an der Ampel zurück.
questions.answer: Gehen Sie an der Ampel nach rechts.
questions.explanation: Rechts means right, links means left, and nach rechts is the normal way to say turn right.

questions.prompt: Complete with the short form: 'Wie komme ich ___ Apotheke?'
questions.explanation: Apotheke is feminine, so zu der is required, and zu der is shortened to zur.

questions.prompt: Complete with the word that means neither left nor right but forwards: 'Gehen Sie hier ___.'
questions.explanation: Geradeaus is the direction word for carrying straight on, and it stands after the place word hier.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'How do I get to the school?'
questions.explanation: The question word comes first, the verb komme second, and zur is the short form of zu der before a feminine noun.

activities.title: Der Weg zum Bahnhof
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A stranger asks you for the station, so give the route step by step.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Entschuldigung, wie komme ich zum Bahnhof?
lines.en: Excuse me, how do I get to the station?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gehen Sie hier geradeaus.
lines.en: Go straight ahead here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und dann?
lines.en: And then?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: An der Ampel gehen Sie nach links.
lines.en: At the traffic lights you go left.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist das weit?
lines.en: Is that far?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, der Bahnhof ist neben der Post.
lines.en: No, the station is next to the post office.

days.title: Verkehrsmittel und die Präposition mit
days.objective: You can say how you travel, using mit plus the dative for every means of transport and zu Fuß for walking.
days.paretoFocus: Every conversation about work, school or a trip contains the question how you get there, and one small pattern with mit covers all of it.
drills: Say how you got to three different places this week.
Ask a friend wie kommst du and answer for yourself with three different vehicles.
List five means of transport with their articles and then put mit in front of each one.

items: Which case follows the preposition mit?
How do you say 'I go on foot' and why is there no article?
How do you say 'We are going by train to Hamburg'?

activities.title: Mit dem Bus zur Arbeit
activities.intro: The preposition mit always takes the dative, so der Bus becomes mit dem Bus and die Bahn becomes mit der Bahn. Use it for every vehicle. Walking is the exception: it is zu Fuß, with no article at all.

items.hr: Ich fahre jeden Tag mit dem Bus zur Arbeit.
items.en: I go to work by bus every day.
items.note: der Bus -> mit dem Bus

items.hr: Meine Schwester fährt mit dem Fahrrad.
items.en: My sister goes by bike.
items.note: das Fahrrad -> mit dem Fahrrad

items.hr: Die Straßenbahn ist schnell. Wir fahren immer mit der Straßenbahn.
items.en: The tram is fast. We always travel by tram.
items.note: die Straßenbahn -> mit der Straßenbahn

items.hr: Zum Supermarkt gehe ich zu Fuß.
items.en: I walk to the supermarket.
items.note: zu Fuß has no article and uses gehen, not fahren

items.hr: Fährst du mit dem Auto oder mit der Bahn?
items.en: Are you going by car or by train?
items.note: das Auto, die Bahn

activities.title: Wie kommst du dahin?
activities.intro: Practise mit plus the dative and the fixed expression for walking.

questions.prompt: Ich fahre ___ Bahn nach Berlin.
questions.explanation: Bahn is feminine, and after mit the feminine article die becomes der.

questions.prompt: Wie kommst du zur Schule? Which answer means that you walk?
questions.explanation: Walking is expressed by the fixed phrase zu Fuß, which takes no article and no mit.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition and the dative article: 'Wir fahren ___ Auto nach Hamburg.'
questions.explanation: Auto is neuter, so das becomes dem after mit.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition and the dative article: 'Meine Kollegin kommt ___ Straßenbahn.'
questions.explanation: Straßenbahn is feminine, so die becomes der after mit.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I travel to work by tram.'
questions.explanation: The verb fahre is second, mit plus the feminine dative der names the means of transport and zur Arbeit gives the destination.

activities.title: Mit dem Bus oder zu Fuß?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks how you travel, so answer with mit plus the dative and with zu Fuß.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie kommst du zur Arbeit?
lines.en: How do you get to work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich fahre mit dem Bus.
lines.en: I go by bus.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fährst du nie mit dem Fahrrad?
lines.en: Do you never go by bike?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Doch, im Sommer fahre ich oft mit dem Fahrrad.
lines.en: Yes I do, in summer I often go by bike.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und heute?
lines.en: And today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Heute gehe ich zu Fuß. Das Wetter ist gut.
lines.en: Today I am walking. The weather is good.

days.title: Der Dativ als drittes Element
days.objective: You can say to whom you give, show, bring or write something, using dative nouns and the dative pronouns mir, dir, ihm, ihr, uns, euch and ihnen.
days.paretoFocus: Verbs like geben, bringen and zeigen need a second person in the sentence, and without the dative you cannot say who receives anything.
drills: Say three sentences with geben in which you name both the receiver and the thing.
Recite the dative pronouns for ich, du, er, sie, wir and ihr from memory.
Turn three of your sentences around so that the receiver becomes a pronoun.

items: Which question word asks for the dative, and which one asks for the accusative?
What are the dative forms of ich, du and er?
When both objects are nouns, which one comes first?

activities.title: Wem gibst du das?
activities.intro: Verbs like geben, bringen, zeigen, schenken and schreiben can carry two objects: the receiver in the dative and the thing in the accusative. Ask wem for the receiver and was for the thing. When both are nouns, the dative noun comes first, and the possessive mein takes the ending em or er in the dative.

items.hr: Ich gebe dem Kind den Ball.
items.en: I am giving the child the ball.
items.note: wem? dem Kind. was? den Ball

items.hr: Der Kellner bringt der Frau einen Kaffee.
items.en: The waiter is bringing the woman a coffee.
items.note: die Frau -> der Frau im Dativ

items.hr: Ich schenke meinem Bruder ein Buch.
items.en: I am giving my brother a book as a present.
items.note: mein -> meinem before a masculine noun

items.hr: Kannst du mir bitte das Salz geben?
items.en: Can you pass me the salt, please?
items.note: ich -> mir

items.en: We are showing you the flat.
items.note: ihr -> euch; du -> dir, er -> ihm, sie -> ihr

activities.title: Wem oder was?
activities.intro: Practise the dative of the receiver next to the accusative of the thing.

questions.prompt: Ich schenke ___ Bruder ein Buch.
questions.explanation: Bruder is the receiver, so it stands in the dative, and mein takes the ending em before a masculine noun.

questions.prompt: Der Kellner bringt ___ Frau einen Tee.
questions.explanation: Frau is the receiver, so the feminine article die becomes der in the dative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative pronoun that stands for ich: 'Gib ___ bitte das Buch.'
questions.explanation: The receiver is the speaker, and the dative form of ich is mir.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative article: 'Wir zeigen ___ Kindern die Stadt.'
questions.explanation: The plural article die becomes den in the dative, and the noun itself already carries the extra n in Kindern.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am giving the child the apple.'
questions.explanation: With two nouns the receiver in the dative comes before the thing in the accusative.

activities.title: Geschenke für die Familie
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your presents, so answer with a receiver in the dative and a thing in the accusative.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was schenkst du deiner Schwester?
lines.en: What are you giving your sister?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich schenke ihr ein Buch.
lines.en: I am giving her a book.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und deinem Vater?
lines.en: And your father?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Meinem Vater kaufe ich eine Uhr.
lines.en: I am buying my father a watch.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zeigst du mir die Geschenke?
lines.en: Will you show me the presents?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich zeige dir alles heute Abend.
lines.en: Yes, I will show you everything this evening.

days.title: Verben mit Dativ, helfen, gefallen und schmecken
days.objective: You can use the dative verbs helfen, gefallen, schmecken and gehören, including the German way of saying that you like something.
days.paretoFocus: Gefallen and schmecken are how Germans really express liking a thing or a dish, and the sentence works the other way round from English, so it needs practice.
drills: Say what three things in your home please you, using gefallen.
Comment on two dishes with schmecken and the right dative pronoun.
Offer help to three different people, changing the dative each time.

items: In Die Wohnung gefällt mir, what is the subject of the sentence?
Which case follows helfen, and how do you say 'I am helping my mother'?
How do you say 'The shoes do not please us' in German?

activities.title: Das gefällt mir
activities.intro: A small group of very common verbs takes the dative instead of the accusative: helfen, gefallen, schmecken, gehören and danken. With gefallen and schmecken the thing is the subject, so the verb agrees with the thing and the person stands in the dative. That is why Die Wohnung gefällt mir literally says the flat pleases me.

items.en: I am helping my mother.
items.note: helfen + Dativ, meine -> meiner

items.en: I like the flat very much.
items.note: die Wohnung is the subject, mir is the person

items.hr: Wie schmeckt dir die Suppe? Sie schmeckt mir gut.
items.en: How do you like the soup? I like it.
items.note: schmecken is only used for food and drink

items.en: The bike belongs to my father.
items.note: gehören + Dativ

items.en: We do not like the shoes.
items.note: plural subject -> gefallen

activities.title: Verben mit Dativ
activities.intro: Practise the verbs that need a person in the dative.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I like the flat' in German?
questions.explanation: The thing is the subject and the person stands in the dative, so mir is correct and mich is wrong.

questions.prompt: Ich helfe ___ Vater.
questions.explanation: Helfen takes the dative, so mein becomes meinem before a masculine noun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the possessive in the dative: 'Das Auto gehört ___ Schwester.'
questions.explanation: Gehören takes the dative, and meine becomes meiner before a feminine noun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of the verb that means to please: 'Die Schuhe ___ mir sehr.'
questions.explanation: The subject is the plural die Schuhe, so the verb takes the plural ending and the person stays in the dative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The soup tastes good to me.'
questions.explanation: Die Suppe is the subject, schmeckt is in second position and mir names the person who tastes it.

activities.title: Wie gefällt dir das?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are a guest at a friend's home, so react with gefallen, schmecken and helfen.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie gefällt dir meine Wohnung?
lines.en: How do you like my flat?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie gefällt mir sehr gut.
lines.en: I like it very much.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wie schmeckt dir der Kuchen?
lines.en: And how do you like the cake?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Er schmeckt mir super. Danke!
lines.en: I think it is great. Thank you!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hilfst du mir später in der Küche?
lines.en: Will you help me in the kitchen later?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Natürlich helfe ich dir.
lines.en: Of course I will help you.

days.title: Personalpronomen im Akkusativ
days.objective: You can replace an accusative noun with mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch or Sie instead of repeating the noun.
days.paretoFocus: Repeating the full noun in every sentence sounds foreign, and the accusative pronouns make your speech shorter and much more natural at once.
drills: Take five sentences with an object and replace each object with a pronoun.
Say three sentences about people you know, using ihn, sie and es correctly.
Ask someone to call you and to visit you, using the right pronoun each time.

items: Which pronoun replaces a masculine noun in the accusative?
What are the accusative forms of ich, du and wir?
How do you say 'I am looking for it' about die Zeitung?

activities.title: Ich kenne ihn
activities.intro: In the accusative the pronouns are mich and dich, then ihn, sie and es matching gender, plus uns, euch and Sie. The pronoun keeps the gender of the noun it replaces, so der Schlüssel becomes ihn and die Zeitung becomes sie. The pronoun stands directly after the conjugated verb, and with a separable verb the prefix still goes to the end.

items.hr: Kennst du Herrn Meier? Ja, ich kenne ihn gut.
items.en: Do you know Mr Meier? Yes, I know him well.
items.note: der Mann -> ihn

items.hr: Wo ist die Zeitung? Ich suche sie.
items.en: Where is the newspaper? I am looking for it.
items.note: die Zeitung -> sie

items.hr: Das Buch ist toll. Ich lese es gerade.
items.en: The book is great. I am reading it right now.
items.note: das Buch -> es

items.hr: Ruf mich bitte heute Abend an!
items.en: Please call me this evening!
items.note: anrufen is separable, ich -> mich

items.en: We are visiting you on Sunday.
items.note: ihr -> euch, wir -> uns

activities.title: Pronomen statt Nomen
activities.intro: Practise replacing accusative nouns with the right pronoun.

questions.prompt: Wo ist der Schlüssel? Ich suche ___.
questions.explanation: Schlüssel is masculine and it is the object of suchen, so the accusative pronoun ihn replaces it.

questions.prompt: Replace das Kind: 'Ich hole ___ um vier Uhr ab.'
questions.explanation: Kind is neuter, and the neuter pronoun es is the same in the nominative and the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative pronoun that stands for du: 'Ich verstehe ___ nicht.'
questions.explanation: Verstehen takes an accusative object, and the accusative of du is dich.

questions.prompt: Complete with a pronoun instead of the noun: 'Die Blumen sind schön. Ich kaufe ___.'
questions.explanation: Blumen is plural, and the plural accusative pronoun is sie, the same form as the nominative.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Please call me tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: The imperative comes first, the pronoun mich follows the verb at once, and the separable prefix an closes the sentence.

activities.title: Wo ist mein Handy?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Your partner cannot find things, so answer using accusative pronouns instead of the nouns.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich finde mein Handy nicht. Siehst du es?
lines.en: I cannot find my phone. Can you see it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, ich sehe es nicht.
lines.en: No, I cannot see it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und meine Brille? Hast du sie?
lines.en: And my glasses? Do you have them?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe sie hier.
lines.en: Yes, I have them here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Rufst du mich später an?
lines.en: Great. Will you call me later?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Klar, ich rufe dich um acht Uhr an.
lines.en: Sure, I will call you at eight o'clock.

days.title: Das Perfekt mit haben
days.objective: You can talk about the past with haben plus a participle at the end of the sentence.
days.paretoFocus: The perfect is the past tense Germans actually speak, so this one pattern unlocks every story about yesterday, last week or your holiday.
drills: Say three things you did yesterday, each with haben and a participle.
Ask a friend two questions about the weekend in the perfect.
Take three present tense sentences you know and move them into the perfect.

items: Where does the participle stand in a normal statement?
Which verb is conjugated in the perfect, and which one never changes?
How do you ask 'What did you do on Saturday?'

activities.title: Was hast du gemacht?
activities.intro: The perfect is built from a conjugated form of haben in second position and a participle at the very end. This is the bracket you already know from the modal verbs and the separable verbs. In a yes or no question the form of haben moves to the front, and the participle still closes the sentence.

items.en: I cooked pizza yesterday.
items.note: habe in position two, gekocht at the end

items.hr: Was hast du am Samstag gemacht?
items.en: What did you do on Saturday?
items.note: question word first, hast second

items.en: We watched a film.
items.note: the object stands between the two verb parts

items.en: She worked for a long time.
items.note: sie -> hat

items.en: Have you eaten already?
items.note: yes or no question: habt comes first

activities.title: Die Satzklammer im Perfekt
activities.intro: Practise the position of haben and of the participle.

questions.prompt: In a normal German statement in the perfect, where does the participle stand?
questions.explanation: The conjugated form of haben takes second position and the participle closes the sentence, forming the verb bracket.

questions.prompt: Ich ___ gestern viel gelernt.
questions.explanation: The subject is ich, so haben appears as habe, and lernen forms its perfect with haben.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of haben: 'Was ___ du am Wochenende gemacht?'
questions.explanation: The subject is du, so the form is hast, and it stands in second position after the question word.

questions.prompt: Put the missing verb form of sehen at the end: 'Wir haben einen Film ___.'
questions.explanation: The participle closes the sentence, and sehen forms it with ge and the ending en.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Yesterday I cooked a soup.'
questions.explanation: Gestern occupies the first position, so habe follows immediately and the subject ich comes after it, with the participle at the end.

activities.title: Was hast du gestern gemacht?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your evening, so answer in the perfect with haben.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was hast du gestern gemacht?
lines.en: What did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe zu Hause gearbeitet.
lines.en: I worked at home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Habt ihr am Abend gekocht?
lines.en: Did you cook in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, wir haben eine Suppe gekocht.
lines.en: Yes, we cooked a soup.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und danach?
lines.en: And after that?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danach haben wir einen Film gesehen.
lines.en: After that we watched a film.

days.title: Das Partizip II der regelmäßigen Verben
days.objective: You can build the participle of regular verbs with ge and t, including the separable verbs and the two groups that take no ge at all.
days.paretoFocus: Most German verbs are regular, so one clear formula plus two exceptions gives you the past form of hundreds of words.
drills: Build the participle of ten regular verbs you already know and say them out loud.
Write three sentences in the perfect with separable verbs.
Find two verbs ending in ieren and use them in the perfect.

items: What is the formula for the participle of a regular verb?
Where does ge go in a separable verb like einkaufen?
Which two groups of verbs never take ge?

activities.title: Regelmäßige Partizipien
activities.intro: A regular verb forms its participle as ge plus the stem plus t, so machen becomes gemacht. If the stem ends in t or d, an extra e is inserted, as in gearbeitet. Separable verbs put ge between the prefix and the stem, while verbs ending in ieren and verbs with the inseparable prefixes be, ver and er drop the ge entirely.

items.en: I did the homework.
items.note: machen -> gemacht, ge + Stamm + t

items.en: I worked for a long time yesterday.
items.note: arbeiten -> gearbeitet, extra e after t or d

items.en: We did the shopping at the supermarket.
items.note: einkaufen -> eingekauft, ge sits in the middle

items.en: She studied medicine.
items.note: verbs in ieren take no ge

items.en: I visited my grandmother.
items.note: be, ver and er are inseparable and take no ge

activities.title: Partizip mit ge und t
activities.intro: Practise the regular participle and the cases that drop ge.

questions.prompt: Which form is the participle of telefonieren?
questions.explanation: Verbs ending in ieren never take ge, so only the ending t is added to the stem.

questions.prompt: Which form is the participle of besuchen?
questions.explanation: Be is an inseparable prefix, so no ge is added and the verb simply takes the ending t.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle of kaufen: 'Ich habe ein Kleid ___.'
questions.explanation: Kaufen is regular, so the participle is ge plus the stem plus t.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle of einkaufen: 'Wir haben am Samstag ___.'
questions.explanation: With a separable verb the syllable ge stands between the prefix ein and the stem.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'She studied medicine.'
questions.explanation: Hat is in second position and the participle without ge closes the sentence.

activities.title: Der Samstag
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Talk about your Saturday using regular participles, including a separable verb.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du am Samstag eingekauft?
lines.en: Did you do the shopping on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe im Supermarkt eingekauft.
lines.en: Yes, I did the shopping at the supermarket.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was hast du sonst gemacht?
lines.en: And what else did you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe die Wohnung aufgeräumt.
lines.en: I tidied up the flat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du deine Oma besucht?
lines.en: Did you visit your grandmother?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe sie am Nachmittag besucht.
lines.en: Yes, I visited her in the afternoon.

days.title: Das Partizip II der unregelmäßigen Verben
days.objective: You can form and recognise the participles of the common strong verbs, which end in en and often change their stem vowel.
days.paretoFocus: The verbs you use most often are exactly the irregular ones, so these twenty forms cover most of what you will actually say about the past.
drills: Learn five strong participles as pairs, for example trinken and getrunken.
Say three sentences in the perfect about your last meal.
Check whether the participles you use end in t or in en, and say why.

items: Which ending do the participles of strong verbs take?
What are the participles of essen, trinken and lesen?
Which auxiliary verb goes with sitzen, stehen and liegen in standard German?

activities.title: Starke Partizipien
activities.intro: Strong verbs form the participle with ge plus the stem plus en, and the stem vowel often changes, as in trinken and getrunken. These forms have to be memorised one by one. Note that sitzen, stehen and liegen take haben in standard German, so you say hat gesessen, hat gestanden and hat gelegen.

items.en: I drank a coffee.
items.note: trinken -> getrunken

items.en: We ate at home.
items.note: essen -> gegessen

items.hr: Ich habe meiner Mutter eine E-Mail geschrieben.
items.en: I wrote my mother an email.
items.note: schreiben -> geschrieben

items.hr: Die Katze hat auf dem Sofa gelegen.
items.en: The cat was lying on the sofa.
items.note: liegen -> hat gelegen, with haben

items.hr: Entschuldigung, ich habe dich nicht verstanden.
items.en: Sorry, I did not understand you.
items.note: verstehen -> verstanden, no ge after the prefix ver

activities.title: Unregelmäßige Formen
activities.intro: Practise the participles of the most frequent strong verbs.

questions.prompt: Which form is the participle of trinken?
questions.explanation: Trinken is a strong verb, so the participle ends in en and the stem vowel changes from i to u.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct in standard German?
options: Ich habe auf dem Stuhl gesessen.
Ich bin auf dem Stuhl gesessen.
Ich habe auf dem Stuhl gesitzt.
Ich bin auf dem Stuhl gesitzen.
questions.answer: Ich habe auf dem Stuhl gesessen.
questions.explanation: Sitzen forms its participle as gesessen and takes haben in the standard language.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle of essen: 'Wir haben schon ___.'
questions.explanation: Essen is strong, and its participle keeps the ending en in the irregular form gegessen.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle of schreiben: 'Ich habe ihm eine Karte ___.'
questions.explanation: Schreiben is strong, so the participle ends in en and the stem vowel changes from ei to ie.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I did not understand you.'
questions.explanation: Nicht stands just before the participle, and verstehen takes no ge because ver is inseparable.

activities.title: Am Abend
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Over coffee you recount last night, and strong participles carry every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Was hast du gestern Abend getrunken?
lines.en: What did you drink last night?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich habe nur Wasser getrunken.
lines.en: I only drank water.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was habt ihr gegessen?
lines.en: And what did you eat?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Wir haben Fisch gegessen.
lines.en: We ate fish.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du danach noch gelesen?
lines.en: Did you read anything afterwards?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich habe ein Buch gelesen und dann eine E-Mail geschrieben.
lines.en: Yes, I read a book and then wrote an email.

days.title: Das Perfekt mit sein
days.objective: You can decide when the perfect needs sein instead of haben and use it correctly with verbs of movement and change of state.
days.paretoFocus: Choosing the wrong auxiliary is the mistake listeners notice first, and the rule behind it is short enough to learn in one lesson.
drills: List five verbs that take sein and say one sentence with each.
Describe your last trip in three sentences, choosing haben or sein each time.
Test yourself: for ten verbs, say sein or haben out loud before you check.

items: Which two groups of verbs take sein in the perfect?
Which two verbs take sein even though nothing moves?
How do you say 'We walked home' in the perfect?

activities.title: Perfekt mit sein
activities.intro: Most verbs form the perfect with haben, but two groups take sein: verbs of movement from one place to another, such as gehen, fahren and kommen, and verbs of change of state, such as aufstehen and einschlafen. On top of that, sein and bleiben themselves take sein. Everything else takes haben.

items.hr: Ich bin gestern nach Berlin gefahren.
items.en: I went to Berlin yesterday.
items.note: fahren is movement, so it takes sein

items.hr: Wir sind zu Fuß nach Hause gegangen.
items.en: We walked home.
items.note: gehen -> ist gegangen

items.hr: Sie ist um sechs Uhr aufgestanden.
items.en: She got up at six o'clock.
items.note: aufstehen is a change of state

items.hr: Ich bin am Sonntag zu Hause geblieben.
items.en: I stayed at home on Sunday.
items.note: bleiben takes sein although nothing moves

items.en: Where have you been?
items.note: sein -> ist gewesen

activities.title: Haben oder sein?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right auxiliary verb in the perfect.

questions.prompt: Ich ___ nach Hause gegangen.
questions.explanation: Gehen is a verb of movement from one place to another, so it takes sein, and with ich that form is bin.

questions.prompt: Which of these verbs forms the perfect with sein?
questions.explanation: Kommen expresses movement from one place to another, while the other three describe an activity and therefore take haben.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of sein: 'Wir ___ um acht Uhr aufgestanden.'
questions.explanation: Aufstehen is a change of state, so it takes sein, and with wir the form is sind.

questions.prompt: Complete with the participle of bleiben: 'Ich bin gestern zu Hause ___.'
questions.explanation: Bleiben is a strong verb, so its participle ends in en, and it belongs to the small group that takes sein.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Yesterday I drove home.'
questions.explanation: Gestern takes the first position, so bin follows straight away, and the participle of the movement verb closes the sentence.

activities.title: Der Ausflug
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks about your Sunday trip, so use the perfect with sein.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo bist du am Sonntag gewesen?
lines.en: Where were you on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin nach Köln gefahren.
lines.en: I went to Cologne.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wann bist du aufgestanden?
lines.en: When did you get up?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin um sechs Uhr aufgestanden.
lines.en: I got up at six o'clock.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und wann bist du zurückgekommen?
lines.en: And when did you come back?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Abend. Meine Freundin ist zu Hause geblieben.
lines.en: In the evening. My girlfriend stayed at home.

days.title: Das Präteritum von sein und haben
days.objective: You can use war and hatte to talk about past states, because these two verbs are almost never used in the perfect.
days.paretoFocus: Germans say ich war and ich hatte, not the perfect forms, so without these two short verbs half of your past sentences will sound wrong.
drills: Say where you were on three days last week, using war.
Name three things you had or did not have yesterday, using hatte.
Recite both verbs through all six persons without looking.

items: What are the six forms of sein in the simple past?
What are the six forms of haben in the simple past?
How do you ask a friend where they were at the weekend?

activities.title: War und hatte
activities.intro: For sein and haben, German speakers use the simple past rather than the perfect in everyday speech. The forms of sein are war, warst, war, waren, wart, waren, and the forms of haben are hatte, hattest, hatte, hatten, hattet, hatten. Note that the ich form and the er form are identical in both verbs.

items.en: I was ill yesterday.
items.note: sein -> ich war

items.hr: Wo warst du am Wochenende? Ich war bei meiner Oma.
items.en: Where were you at the weekend? I was at my grandmother's.
items.note: du -> warst; bei + Dativ

items.en: We were at the cinema yesterday.
items.note: wir -> waren, ihr -> wart

items.en: I had no time.
items.note: haben -> ich hatte

items.en: We had a lot of work.
items.note: wir -> hatten, du -> hattest

activities.title: Sein und haben im Präteritum
activities.intro: Practise the simple past forms of the two most frequent German verbs.

questions.prompt: Gestern ___ ich sehr müde.
questions.explanation: Müde sein describes a state in the past, and the simple past of sein with ich is war.

questions.prompt: ___ ihr gestern zu Hause?
questions.explanation: The subject is ihr, and the matching simple past form of sein is wart.

questions.prompt: Complete with the simple past of haben for ich: 'Ich ___ gestern keine Zeit.'
questions.explanation: In everyday German the simple past of haben is used instead of the perfect, and with ich the form is hatte.

questions.prompt: Complete with the simple past of sein for du: 'Wo ___ du gestern Abend?'
questions.explanation: The subject is du, so the form is warst, and it stands in second position after the question word.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We were at the cinema yesterday.'
questions.explanation: Waren is the simple past of sein with wir, and im Kino keeps the dative because it says where you were.

activities.title: Gestern
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend could not reach you yesterday, so explain with war and hatte.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wo warst du gestern? Ich habe dich angerufen.
lines.en: Where were you yesterday? I called you.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich war bei meinen Eltern.
lines.en: I was at my parents' place.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Warst du lange dort?
lines.en: Were you there for a long time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, ich hatte viel Zeit.
lines.en: Yes, I had plenty of time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hattet ihr Besuch?
lines.en: Did you have visitors?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, wir waren allein. Es war sehr ruhig.
lines.en: No, we were alone. It was very quiet.

days.title: Das Wetter
days.objective: You can say what the weather is like today and ask someone else about the weather where they are.
days.paretoFocus: Weather is the default opener in German small talk, so a handful of fixed impersonal phrases lets you start almost any conversation.
drills: Say out loud what the weather is doing right now, in three sentences.
Name the four seasons in German and one typical weather sentence for each.
Look out of the window twice today and describe what you see in one German sentence each time.

items: Which question asks about today's weather?
Which little word starts almost every German weather sentence?
Give the German nouns, with their articles, for sun, rain, snow and wind.

activities.title: Wie ist das Wetter heute?
activities.intro: German weather sentences almost always use the impersonal subject es: es regnet, es schneit. For a description you use es ist plus an adjective. The adjective takes no ending because it comes after the verb sein.

items.en: What is the weather like today?
items.note: Wie ist ...? asks for a description.

items.hr: Es regnet, und es ist kalt.
items.en: It is raining, and it is cold.
items.note: regnen is impersonal: only es regnet, never ich regne.

items.en: Today it is very hot.
items.note: heiß takes ß because ei is a diphthong.

items.hr: Im Winter schneit es hier oft.
items.en: In winter it often snows here.
items.note: im Winter, im Sommer, im Herbst, im Frühling.

items.hr: Die Sonne scheint und der Himmel ist blau.
items.en: The sun is shining and the sky is blue.
items.note: die Sonne, der Himmel, der Regen, der Schnee, der Wind.

activities.title: Das Wetter beschreiben
activities.intro: Practise impersonal weather sentences and the most common weather words.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'It is snowing today'?
questions.explanation: The conjugated verb stays in position two, so after Heute comes schneit and only then the subject es. German never drops the es here.

questions.prompt: Complete in standard German of Germany: 'Im Sommer ist es oft ___.'
questions.explanation: Standard German writes ß after a long vowel or a diphthong, so heiß. Adjectives are also written in lower case.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap: 'Wie ___ das Wetter heute?'
questions.explanation: Wie asks for a description and takes the verb sein in the third person singular.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap: 'Nimm einen Schirm, es ___ draußen.'
questions.explanation: The verb regnen is impersonal and takes the ending t after es.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Today the sun is shining and it is warm.'
questions.explanation: Heute stands in position one, so the verb scheint must be in position two and the subject die Sonne follows it.

activities.title: Regen am Morgen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend compare the weather in your two towns using impersonal es sentences.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Morgen! Wie ist das Wetter bei dir?
lines.en: Good morning! What is the weather like where you are?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Guten Morgen! Hier regnet es seit sieben Uhr.
lines.en: Good morning! It has been raining here since seven o'clock.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bei mir auch. Es ist kalt und der Wind ist stark.
lines.en: Here too. It is cold and the wind is strong.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gestern war es noch schön und warm.
lines.en: Yesterday it was still nice and warm.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, der Sommer kommt langsam.
lines.en: Yes, summer is coming slowly.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich hoffe es. Heute nehme ich den Schirm mit.
lines.en: I hope so. Today I am taking my umbrella with me.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gute Idee. Bis später!
lines.en: Good idea. See you later!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bis später!
lines.en: See you later!

days.title: Kleidung und Farben
days.objective: You can name everyday clothes with the correct article and say what colour they are.
days.paretoFocus: Clothes plus colours cover shopping, lost property and describing people, and they give you a safe place to use adjectives after sein.
drills: Open your wardrobe and name five items with their article and colour.
Describe what you are wearing right now in three German sentences.
Write down which clothing nouns in your list are plural and check the verb form you need.

items: Which verb means to wear, and how does it change in the du and er forms?
Give the articles for jacket, shirt, dress and skirt in German.
Why is it sind and not ist in 'Meine Schuhe ___ schwarz'?

activities.title: Kleidung und Farben
activities.intro: Colour words are adjectives, and after sein they never take an ending. Learn every clothing noun together with its article, because you will need it when you shop. A few common items are plural in German, and then the verb must be plural too.

items.en: The jacket is blue.
items.note: die Jacke, der Mantel, das Hemd, die Hose.

items.en: What are you wearing today?
items.note: tragen -> du trägst, er trägt, with a becoming ä.

items.hr: Ich trage eine Hose und einen Pullover.
items.en: I am wearing trousers and a jumper.
items.note: die Hose is singular in German; einen Pullover is accusative masculine.

items.hr: Meine Schuhe sind schwarz, meine Socken sind weiß.
items.en: My shoes are black, my socks are white.
items.note: die Schuhe and die Socken are plural, so the verb is sind.

items.hr: Welche Farbe hat der Rock? Er ist grün.
items.en: What colour is the skirt? It is green.
items.note: der Rock -> er, die Bluse -> sie, das Kleid -> es.

activities.title: Farben und Kleidungsstücke
activities.intro: Practise clothing articles, plural verbs and colour adjectives after sein.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct German sentence about a red skirt.
questions.explanation: After the verb sein an adjective takes no ending, and the verb itself stays in position two.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Meine Schuhe ___ neu.'
questions.explanation: die Schuhe is plural, so sein takes the plural form sind. The singular ist would need a singular noun such as der Schuh.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the right article: '___ Kleid ist gelb.'
questions.explanation: Kleid is neuter, so it takes the article das. Here it also opens the sentence and is written with a capital letter.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the correct form of tragen: 'Du ___ heute einen Pullover.'
questions.explanation: tragen is a stem-changing verb: ich trage, but du trägst and er trägt with an umlaut.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My trousers are black and my shirt is white.'
questions.explanation: die Hose is feminine and takes meine, das Hemd is neuter and takes mein. Both colour adjectives stay without an ending after ist.

activities.title: Im Geschäft
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are buying a jacket and have to name the item, the colour and the size.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag, kann ich Ihnen helfen?
lines.en: Hello, can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, bitte. Ich suche eine Jacke.
lines.en: Yes, please. I am looking for a jacket.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Welche Farbe möchten Sie?
lines.en: What colour would you like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Blau, bitte. Diese Jacke hier ist schön.
lines.en: Blue, please. This jacket here is nice.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Die Jacke ist leider zu klein. Hier ist Größe M.
lines.en: That jacket is unfortunately too small. Here is size M.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke. Die ist gut. Was kostet sie?
lines.en: Thank you. That one is good. How much is it?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fünfzig Euro.
lines.en: Fifty euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gut, ich nehme sie.
lines.en: Good, I will take it.

days.title: Adjektive nach dem Verb sein
days.objective: You can describe people, places and things with adjectives placed after sein, without adding any ending.
days.paretoFocus: This is the one adjective pattern you can use safely from day one, because the word never changes no matter what gender or number the noun has.
drills: Describe four objects in your room with sein plus one adjective each.
Take three of those sentences and add sehr, ziemlich or zu to change the strength.
Say three things about yourself right now using ich bin plus an adjective.

items: Does the adjective change after sein if the noun is feminine? Why not?
What is the difference in meaning between sehr, ziemlich and zu?
Where does nicht stand when you negate an adjective after sein?

activities.title: Adjektive nach sein
activities.intro: An adjective that comes after sein, werden or bleiben has no ending at all, whatever the gender or number of the noun. Only adjectives placed directly in front of a noun change, and those come much later. You can adjust the strength with sehr, ziemlich, zu and nicht.

items.en: The coffee is hot.
items.note: Kaffee is masculine, but heiß still has no ending.

items.en: The soup is hot.
items.note: Feminine noun, exactly the same adjective form.

items.en: The room is very small.
items.note: sehr strengthens, ziemlich softens: ziemlich klein.

items.en: My shoes are not expensive.
items.note: nicht stands directly before the adjective.

items.hr: Der Film war langweilig, das Buch ist interessant.
items.en: The film was boring, the book is interesting.
items.note: war is the past form of sein and behaves the same way.

activities.title: Ohne Endung
activities.intro: Practise adjectives after sein and the words that strengthen or negate them.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct German for 'The soup is cold.'
questions.explanation: After sein the adjective keeps its basic form with no ending, and the verb stands in position two.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'The room is too small'?
questions.explanation: zu means too much of something, sehr means very, ziemlich means fairly and nicht negates.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the German adjective meaning 'tired': 'Ich bin heute sehr ___.'
questions.explanation: After sein the adjective stays in its basic form, so no ending is added even with sehr in front of it.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap: 'Meine Wohnung ___ nicht groß.'
questions.explanation: die Wohnung is singular, so sein becomes ist, and nicht then stands directly before the adjective.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'The coffee is not very hot.'
questions.explanation: nicht comes before the strengthening word sehr, and the adjective heiß ends the sentence without an ending.

activities.title: Die Wohnung
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your flat and every answer uses an adjective after sein.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Wie ist die Wohnung?
lines.en: What is the flat like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie ist hell und ruhig.
lines.en: It is bright and quiet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Küche? Ist sie groß?
lines.en: And the kitchen? Is it big?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nein, die Küche ist ziemlich klein.
lines.en: No, the kitchen is fairly small.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ist die Miete teuer?
lines.en: Is the rent expensive?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sie ist nicht billig. Für mich ist sie okay.
lines.en: It is not cheap. For me it is okay.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und die Nachbarn? Sind sie nett?
lines.en: And the neighbours? Are they nice?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, sie sind sehr freundlich.
lines.en: Yes, they are very friendly.

days.title: Der Körper und beim Arzt
days.objective: You can name the main parts of the body and tell a doctor what hurts and how long you have been ill.
days.paretoFocus: A short list of body words plus the tut weh pattern is enough to get help anywhere, and it is one of the few situations where you cannot avoid speaking.
drills: Point at eight parts of your body and say each noun with its article out loud.
Say three complaints using tut weh and three using the Schmerzen pattern.
Practise answering 'Seit wann?' with three different time expressions.

items: How do you say 'my back hurts' and 'my legs hurt'?
Give the male and the female word for doctor in German.
Which question does a doctor use to ask where the pain is?

activities.title: Beim Arzt
activities.intro: To say that something hurts, German uses the verb tun together with weh: der Kopf tut weh. With a plural body part the verb becomes plural: die Füße tun weh. A second common pattern is haben plus a noun ending in Schmerzen.

items.en: My head hurts.
items.note: der Kopf, der Hals, der Bauch, der Rücken, der Fuß.

items.en: I have a sore throat and a fever.
