items.note: die Schmerzen is always plural; das Fieber stands without an article here.

items.en: Where does it hurt?
items.note: The standard question you will hear first.

items.hr: Die Ärztin gibt mir ein Rezept.
items.en: The doctor gives me a prescription.
items.note: der Arzt, die Ärztin; mir is dative, ein Rezept accusative.

items.hr: Ich bin krank und bleibe im Bett.
items.en: I am ill and I am staying in bed.
items.note: krank, gesund and müde take no ending after sein.

activities.title: Wo tut es weh?
activities.intro: Practise the tut weh pattern, body nouns and the two forms of the word for doctor.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'My feet hurt'?
questions.explanation: die Füße is plural, so the possessive is meine and the verb tun takes the plural form.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ich habe ___ und kann fast nicht sprechen.'
questions.explanation: der Hals is the throat, so Halsschmerzen is the complaint that stops you speaking.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the female job title: 'Ich gehe heute zur ___.'
questions.explanation: der Arzt is the male form and die Ärztin the female one. zur is zu plus der, so a feminine dative follows.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap: 'Mein Rücken ___ weh.'
questions.explanation: der Rücken is singular, so the verb tun becomes tut in the third person, and weh stays at the end.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I have a headache and a fever.'
questions.explanation: haben plus a Schmerzen noun is the normal way to name a complaint, and both nouns keep their capital letter.

activities.title: In der Praxis
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe your symptoms and say how long you have had them.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag. Was fehlt Ihnen?
lines.en: Hello. What is wrong with you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Guten Tag. Mein Hals tut sehr weh.
lines.en: Hello. My throat hurts a lot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Haben Sie auch Fieber?
lines.en: Do you have a fever as well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, seit gestern. Ich bin sehr müde.
lines.en: Yes, since yesterday. I am very tired.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Seit wann sind Sie krank?
lines.en: How long have you been ill?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Seit drei Tagen. Mein Kopf tut auch weh.
lines.en: For three days. My head hurts too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sie brauchen ein Medikament und viel Ruhe.
lines.en: You need some medicine and a lot of rest.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vielen Dank, Frau Doktor.
lines.en: Thank you very much, doctor.

days.title: Hobbys und Freizeit
days.objective: You can talk about your free time, say how often you do something and ask others about their weekend.
days.paretoFocus: Free-time talk is the second question after your job, and it gives you a natural way to keep any conversation going.
drills: List five things you do in your free time, each in one German sentence.
Add a frequency word such as oft, manchmal or nie to each of those sentences.
Ask three different free-time questions out loud, then answer them yourself.

items: Which verb goes with Sport and which one with Fußball?
How do you say 'I go swimming' with two verbs?
Give the du and er forms of lesen.

activities.title: Hobbys und Freizeit
activities.intro: Free-time verbs behave differently from English. A named game takes spielen, general exercise takes machen, and many activities are expressed with gehen plus an infinitive. Frequency words such as oft, manchmal and nie normally stand after the verb.

items.hr: In meiner Freizeit spiele ich Fußball.
items.en: In my free time I play football.
items.note: die Freizeit; Fußball takes ß after a long u.

items.en: At the weekend I go swimming.
items.note: gehen plus infinitive: schwimmen gehen, tanzen gehen, einkaufen gehen.

items.hr: Ich lese jeden Abend ein Buch.
items.en: I read a book every evening.
items.note: lesen -> du liest, er liest, with e becoming ie.

items.hr: Meine Schwester macht dreimal pro Woche Sport.
items.en: My sister does sport three times a week.
items.note: der Sport goes with machen, never with spielen.

items.en: What are you doing on Sunday?
items.note: The present tense also covers plans in the near future.

activities.title: Freizeit
activities.intro: Practise free-time verbs, their endings and the adverbs of frequency.

questions.prompt: Which verb goes with the noun Sport in German?
questions.explanation: German says Sport machen. spielen is used with a named game, for example Fußball spielen or Karten spielen.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Am Samstag ___ ich mit Freunden ins Kino.'
questions.explanation: The subject is ich, so gehen takes the ending e, and the verb stays in position two after Am Samstag.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the correct form of lesen: 'Du ___ sehr viele Bücher.'
questions.explanation: lesen changes e to ie in the du and er forms, and the du ending fuses with the stem: du liest.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the missing noun: 'In meiner ___ koche ich viel.'
questions.explanation: The German word for free time is feminine, which is why it follows meiner, and like every German noun it keeps its capital letter.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'At the weekend I go swimming.'
questions.explanation: Am Wochenende fills position one, so the conjugated verb gehe comes second and the infinitive schwimmen goes to the end.

activities.title: Pläne für das Wochenende
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague compare free-time plans using spielen, machen and gehen.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hast du am Wochenende schon etwas vor?
lines.en: Do you already have plans for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Samstag spiele ich Fußball mit Freunden.
lines.en: On Saturday I am playing football with friends.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und am Sonntag?
lines.en: And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am Sonntag gehe ich schwimmen. Und du?
lines.en: On Sunday I am going swimming. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich mache Sport im Park und lese viel.
lines.en: I do sport in the park and read a lot.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Was liest du gerade?
lines.en: What are you reading at the moment?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Einen Krimi. Er ist sehr spannend.
lines.en: A crime novel. It is very exciting.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Klingt gut. Vielleicht komme ich in den Park mit.
lines.en: Sounds good. Maybe I will come along to the park.

days.title: gern, lieber und am liebsten
days.objective: You can say what you like doing, what you prefer and what you like most, using gern, lieber and am liebsten.
days.paretoFocus: German has no everyday verb for liking an activity, so this little word is the only way to express preference, and it appears in almost every conversation about food, free time and travel.
drills: Say three things you like doing with gern placed right after the verb.
Compare two drinks and two foods using lieber ... als.
Finish the sentence 'Am liebsten ...' three times, keeping the verb in position two.

items: Where exactly does gern stand in a normal sentence?
What are the three steps of the series gern, ..., ...?
How do you say that you do not like doing something?

activities.title: gern, lieber, am liebsten
activities.intro: To say you like doing something, German adds gern to the ordinary verb: ich koche gern. The three-step series is gern, lieber, am liebsten. The word normally stands right after the conjugated verb and its subject.

items.en: I like drinking coffee.
items.note: gern follows the verb, never precedes it.

items.en: I prefer drinking tea.
items.note: lieber compares two things and takes als for the other one.

items.en: Most of all I like drinking water.
items.note: am liebsten in position one pushes the verb into position two.

items.hr: Meine Mutter fährt nicht gern Auto.
items.en: My mother does not like driving.
items.note: The negative is nicht gern, in that order.

items.hr: Was machst du am liebsten am Wochenende?
items.en: What do you most like doing at the weekend?
items.note: A very common question to keep a conversation going.

activities.title: Vorlieben ausdrücken
activities.intro: Practise the three steps of gern and where the word stands in the sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'I prefer tea to coffee'?
options: Ich trinke gern Tee als Kaffee.
Ich trinke lieber Tee als Kaffee.
Ich trinke am liebsten Tee als Kaffee.
Ich trinke Tee gern als Kaffee.
questions.answer: Ich trinke lieber Tee als Kaffee.
questions.explanation: lieber is the middle step of the series and takes als in front of the thing you like less.

questions.prompt: Where does the word gern usually stand in a German sentence?
options: at the very front of the sentence
directly after the conjugated verb and its subject
at the end after a comma
questions.answer: directly after the conjugated verb and its subject
questions.explanation: In ich koche gern the word follows verb and subject and comes before any further information.

questions.prompt: Fill the two-word superlative into the gap: 'Ich esse gern Fisch, aber ___ esse ich Nudeln.'
questions.explanation: The series runs gern, lieber, am liebsten, and the superlative here opens the clause, so the verb esse follows it immediately.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with one word: 'Mein Bruder spielt nicht ___ Karten.'
questions.explanation: The negative of liking an activity is nicht plus this word, in exactly that order.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'Most of all I like reading in the evening.'
questions.explanation: The superlative fills position one as a single unit, so the verb lese must stand in position two and the subject follows it.

activities.title: Kaffee oder Tee?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Every one of your answers uses gern, lieber or am liebsten.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Trinkst du gern Kaffee?
lines.en: Do you like drinking coffee?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber ich trinke lieber Tee.
lines.en: Yes, but I prefer tea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was isst du am liebsten zum Frühstück?
lines.en: And what do you most like eating for breakfast?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Am liebsten esse ich Brot mit Käse.
lines.en: Most of all I like eating bread with cheese.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich koche nicht gern. Und du?
lines.en: I do not like cooking. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich koche sehr gern, besonders am Wochenende.
lines.en: I really like cooking, especially at the weekend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann kochst du am Sonntag für uns?
lines.en: So will you cook for us on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gern! Ich mache eine Suppe.
lines.en: With pleasure! I will make a soup.

days.title: Berufe und Arbeit
days.objective: You can say what you do for a living, name the male and female form of a job, and say where you work.
days.paretoFocus: The job question comes up within a minute of meeting anyone, and the rule that professions take no article after sein removes the most common beginner mistake.
drills: Say your own profession in two ways: with von Beruf and with als.
Write down six jobs, each with the male and the female form and their articles.
Say where three people you know work, using bei or in plus the dative.

items: Why is 'Ich bin ein Lehrer' wrong?
Which ending usually turns a male job title into its female counterpart?
Which preposition do you use for the employer you work for?

activities.title: Berufe
activities.intro: When you simply state your profession after sein, German uses no article at all. Almost every job has a male and a female form, and the female one adds the ending in. To say where you work you use bei plus dative for the employer and in plus dative for the place.

items.en: I am a teacher by profession.
items.note: der Lehrer, die Lehrerin. No article after sein.

items.en: My sister is a doctor.
items.note: der Arzt -> die Ärztin, with an umlaut.

items.hr: Er arbeitet als Verkäufer in einem Supermarkt.
items.en: He works as a shop assistant in a supermarket.
items.note: der Verkäufer, die Verkäuferin; als plus job name.

items.hr: Sie arbeitet bei einer Firma in Hamburg.
items.en: She works for a company in Hamburg.
items.note: bei plus dative: bei einer Firma, bei der Post.

items.hr: Was sind Sie von Beruf? Ich bin Krankenpfleger.
items.en: What is your profession? I am a nurse.
items.note: der Krankenpfleger, die Krankenpflegerin.

activities.title: Was sind Sie von Beruf?
activities.intro: Practise stating a profession, forming the female version and choosing the right preposition.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly states a profession after the verb sein?
options: Ich bin der Lehrer von Beruf.
questions.explanation: A profession named after sein takes no article in German, unlike in English where you must say a teacher.

questions.prompt: What is the female form of der Verkäufer?
questions.explanation: The female form adds the ending in to the male form and always takes the article die.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the female form of der Kellner: 'Meine Freundin ist ___.'
questions.explanation: Add the ending in to the male form and keep the capital letter, because it is still a noun.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with a preposition: 'Sie hat einen Job ___ einer Bank.'
questions.explanation: The employer you work for takes this preposition plus the dative, which is why the article is einer.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'My brother works as a cook in a restaurant.'
questions.explanation: als introduces the job name without an article, and in plus dative gives einem Restaurant for the place of work.

activities.title: Beim Kennenlernen
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You exchange jobs and workplaces with someone you have just met.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und was machen Sie beruflich?
lines.en: And what do you do for a living?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich bin Krankenpfleger. Ich arbeite in einem Krankenhaus.
lines.en: I am a nurse. I work in a hospital.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Interessant. Arbeiten Sie auch am Wochenende?
lines.en: Interesting. Do you work at weekends too?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, manchmal. Und Sie? Was sind Sie von Beruf?
lines.en: Yes, sometimes. And you? What is your profession?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich bin Lehrerin. Ich unterrichte Mathematik.
lines.en: I am a teacher. I teach mathematics.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Macht Ihnen die Arbeit Spaß?
lines.en: Do you enjoy the work?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja, sehr. Die Kinder sind toll.
lines.en: Yes, very much. The children are great.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Das freut mich.
lines.en: I am glad to hear it.

days.title: Die Konjunktionen und, aber, oder und denn
days.objective: You can join two full sentences with und, aber, oder and denn while keeping the verb in position two in both halves.
days.paretoFocus: These four words turn short beginner sentences into adult-sounding speech, and denn lets you give reasons without touching word order at all.
drills: Join three pairs of your own sentences with und, aber and oder.
Give three reasons for things you did today, each with denn.
Read your denn sentences aloud and check that the verb is still the second element.

items: Which of the four words expresses a contrast and which one gives a reason?
After denn, what comes first: the subject or the verb?
Do these four conjunctions count as position one in the clause?

activities.title: und, aber, oder, denn
activities.intro: These four words connect two complete main clauses and change nothing about the word order. The conjugated verb still stands in position two of the second clause. Importantly, the conjunction itself does not count as position one.

items.hr: Ich koche, und meine Frau deckt den Tisch.
items.en: I cook, and my wife sets the table.
items.note: After und the second clause begins normally: subject, then verb.

items.hr: Ich bin müde, aber ich gehe trotzdem laufen.
items.en: I am tired, but I am going running anyway.
items.note: aber marks a contrast.

items.hr: Trinkst du Kaffee, oder möchtest du Tee?
items.en: Are you drinking coffee, or would you like tea?
items.note: Here both halves are questions, so both start with the verb.

items.hr: Ich bleibe heute zu Hause, denn ich bin krank.
items.en: I am staying at home today, because I am ill.
items.note: denn gives a reason and leaves ich bin untouched.

items.hr: Er lernt Deutsch, denn er arbeitet bald in Berlin.
items.en: He is learning German, because he will soon be working in Berlin.
items.note: None of these four words ever moves the verb.

activities.title: Sätze verbinden
activities.intro: Practise choosing between the four connectors without letting them disturb the word order.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct word order after denn?
options: Ich esse jetzt, denn ich Hunger habe.
Ich esse jetzt, denn habe ich Hunger.
Ich esse jetzt, denn ich habe Hunger.
Ich esse jetzt, denn hungrig ich bin.
questions.answer: Ich esse jetzt, denn ich habe Hunger.
questions.explanation: denn joins two main clauses, so the second clause keeps its normal order with the subject first and the verb in position two.

questions.prompt: Which of these four words expresses a contrast?
questions.explanation: aber marks a contrast, und simply adds, oder offers a choice and denn gives a reason.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the connector that gives a reason: 'Ich nehme den Bus, ___ mein Auto ist kaputt.'
questions.explanation: This connector introduces a reason and leaves the word order of the second clause completely unchanged.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with one connector: 'Möchtest du Wasser ___ Saft?'
questions.explanation: This word offers an alternative between two possibilities.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I am staying at home, because I am ill.' Use the connector that leaves the word order unchanged.
questions.explanation: After this connector the second clause runs subject then verb, so bin stays in position two and krank ends the sentence.

activities.title: Eine Verabredung
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Every turn joins two clauses with und, aber, oder or denn.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kommst du heute Abend mit ins Kino?
lines.en: Are you coming to the cinema with us this evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich möchte gern, aber ich habe keine Zeit.
lines.en: I would like to, but I have no time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Schade. Arbeitest du wieder lange?
lines.en: What a pity. Are you working late again?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, denn ich habe morgen einen Termin.
lines.en: Yes, because I have an appointment tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dann gehen wir am Freitag, oder passt Samstag besser?
lines.en: Then we will go on Friday, or does Saturday suit you better?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Freitag ist gut, und danach essen wir eine Pizza.
lines.en: Friday is good, and afterwards we will eat a pizza.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfekt, ich hole dich ab.
lines.en: Perfect, I will pick you up.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danke, bis Freitag!
lines.en: Thanks, see you on Friday!

days.title: Der Nebensatz mit weil
days.objective: You can give reasons with weil and put the conjugated verb at the very end of the weil clause.
days.paretoFocus: weil is the first subordinate clause you learn, and the verb-at-the-end rule it teaches applies later to dass, wenn and every other subordinate clause in German.
drills: Answer the question 'Warum lernst du Deutsch?' three different ways with weil.
Take three of your denn sentences and rewrite each one with weil, moving the verb.
Say one weil sentence containing a modal verb and check that the modal is last.

items: Where does the conjugated verb go in a weil clause?
Rewrite 'denn ich bin müde' with weil.
In a weil clause with a modal verb, which verb comes last?

activities.title: Der Nebensatz mit weil
activities.intro: weil also gives a reason, but it opens a subordinate clause and sends the conjugated verb to the very end. Compare it directly with denn, which leaves the verb in position two. A comma always stands in front of weil.

items.hr: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
items.en: I am staying at home because I am ill.
items.note: Compare denn ich bin krank. With weil the verb bin goes last.

items.hr: Sie lernt Deutsch, weil sie in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
items.en: She is learning German because she wants to work in Germany.
items.note: With a modal verb the modal itself, here möchte, stands last of all.

items.hr: Wir nehmen den Zug, weil das Auto kaputt ist.
items.en: We are taking the train because the car is broken.
items.note: ist moves behind the adjective kaputt.

items.hr: Warum kommst du nicht? Weil ich keine Zeit habe.
items.en: Why are you not coming? Because I have no time.
items.note: A weil clause on its own is normal in speech, and habe is still last.

items.hr: Ich stehe früh auf, weil der Unterricht um acht Uhr beginnt.
items.en: I get up early because the lesson starts at eight o'clock.
items.note: A separable verb stays in one piece at the end: ... weil er um acht aufsteht.

activities.title: Das Verb am Ende
activities.intro: Practise the verb-final rule and the contrast with the connector that does not move the verb.

questions.prompt: Which sentence with weil is correct?
options: Ich gehe früh ins Bett, weil ich bin müde.
Ich gehe früh ins Bett, weil bin ich müde.
Ich gehe früh ins Bett, weil ich müde bin.
Ich gehe früh ins Bett, weil müde ich bin.
questions.answer: Ich gehe früh ins Bett, weil ich müde bin.
questions.explanation: weil opens a subordinate clause and pushes the conjugated verb bin to the very end, behind the adjective.

questions.prompt: Which version keeps the verb in position two of the second clause?
options: Ich koche, weil ich Hunger habe.
Ich koche, denn ich habe Hunger.
Ich koche, weil habe ich Hunger.
Ich koche, denn ich Hunger habe.
questions.answer: Ich koche, denn ich habe Hunger.
questions.explanation: denn joins two main clauses and leaves the verb in position two. Only weil moves it to the end of the clause.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the missing modal verb: 'Ich trinke keinen Kaffee, weil ich dann nicht schlafen ___.'
questions.explanation: In a weil clause the conjugated verb comes last of all, so the modal stands behind the infinitive schlafen.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the connector that sends the verb to the end: 'Er geht zum Arzt, ___ er Fieber hat.'
questions.explanation: This connector opens a subordinate clause, which is exactly why hat has moved behind Fieber.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'We are taking the train because the car is broken.'
questions.explanation: The main clause keeps the verb in position two, and in the weil clause ist is pushed to the very last position.

activities.title: Warum lernst du Deutsch?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Every answer you give contains a weil clause with the verb at the end.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Warum lernst du eigentlich Deutsch?
lines.en: Why are you actually learning German?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten möchte.
lines.en: I am learning German because I want to work in Germany.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Und warum kommst du nie mit ins Café?
lines.en: And why do you never come along to the cafe?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Weil ich abends immer Unterricht habe.
lines.en: Because I always have classes in the evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Am Samstag hast du frei, oder?
lines.en: You are free on Saturday, aren't you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja, aber ich schlafe lange, weil die Woche sehr anstrengend ist.
lines.en: Yes, but I sleep in, because the week is very tiring.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Verstehe. Dann treffen wir uns am Sonntag.
lines.en: I see. Then we will meet on Sunday.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sehr gern, weil ich sonntags immer Zeit habe.
lines.en: Gladly, because I always have time on Sundays.

days.title: Sich vorstellen, das Prüfungsgespräch üben
days.objective: You can introduce yourself in seven fixed steps and then ask and answer one simple question on an everyday topic.
days.paretoFocus: This is the exact shape of the beginner speaking test and of every first meeting in real life, so one rehearsed script covers both.
drills: Say your introduction out loud in the fixed order: name, age, country, town, languages, job, hobby.
Time yourself and keep the whole introduction under one minute.
Prepare three simple everyday questions and answer each of them in one sentence.

items: Which verb does German use for age, and what is the full sentence pattern?
What is the difference between woher, wo and wohin?
Name the seven pieces of information in a short self-introduction.

activities.title: Sich vorstellen
activities.intro: In a short spoken introduction you give seven pieces of information in a fixed order: name, age, country, place of residence, languages, job and hobby. Each one is a single simple sentence. Afterwards you ask your partner one everyday question and answer theirs.

items.hr: Ich heiße Marco Silva und ich bin dreißig Jahre alt.
items.en: My name is Marco Silva and I am thirty years old.
items.note: heißen: ich heiße, du heißt, er heißt. Age uses sein, not haben.

items.hr: Ich komme aus Portugal und wohne jetzt in Köln.
items.en: I come from Portugal and now live in Cologne.
items.note: kommen aus for the country, wohnen in for the town.

items.hr: Ich spreche Portugiesisch, Englisch und ein bisschen Deutsch.
items.en: I speak Portuguese, English and a little German.
items.note: Language names are nouns here and keep their capital letter; bisschen has ss after a short i.

items.hr: Ich bin Kellner von Beruf, und meine Hobbys sind Kochen und Fußball.
items.en: I am a waiter by profession, and my hobbies are cooking and football.
items.note: das Hobby, die Hobbys; der Kellner, die Kellnerin.

items.hr: Und Sie? Wie oft kaufen Sie im Supermarkt ein?
items.en: And you? How often do you shop at the supermarket?
items.note: einkaufen is separable, so ein goes to the end: Sie kaufen ein.

activities.title: Das Gespräch üben
activities.intro: Practise the sentences of a self-introduction and the question words you need to reply.

questions.prompt: Which sentence states your age correctly in German?
options: Mein Alter ist dreißig Jahre alt.
questions.explanation: German uses sein for age, not haben, and the word alt is part of the fixed pattern.

questions.prompt: Which question asks where someone lives?
questions.explanation: wo asks about a place where you are, woher about origin and wohin about a destination.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the verb of origin: 'Ich ___ aus Spanien.'
questions.explanation: The verb kommen plus aus states your country of origin, and with ich it takes the ending e.

questions.prompt: Fill the gap with the correct form: 'Wie ___ du mit Nachnamen?'
questions.explanation: The verb heißen keeps its ß, and in the du form the ending fuses with the stem, so no extra s is added.

questions.prompt: Build the German for: 'I speak German and a little English.'
questions.explanation: ein bisschen is a fixed unit that stands directly in front of the language it limits, and both language names are capitalised.

activities.title: Wir stellen uns vor
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Give the seven pieces of your introduction as they are asked for, then ask one question back.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Guten Tag. Wie heißen Sie?
lines.en: Hello. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Guten Tag. Ich heiße Marco Silva und ich bin dreißig Jahre alt.
lines.en: Hello. My name is Marco Silva and I am thirty years old.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Woher kommen Sie und wo wohnen Sie jetzt?
lines.en: Where do you come from and where do you live now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ich komme aus Portugal und wohne seit einem Jahr in Köln.
lines.en: I come from Portugal and have lived in Cologne for a year.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Welche Sprachen sprechen Sie?
lines.en: Which languages do you speak?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Portugiesisch, Englisch und ein bisschen Deutsch. Was sind Sie von Beruf?
lines.en: Portuguese, English and a little German. What is your profession?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ich bin Lehrerin. Und wie oft kaufen Sie ein?
lines.en: I am a teacher. And how often do you go shopping?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zweimal pro Woche, weil ich gern frisch koche.
lines.en: Twice a week, because I like cooking with fresh food.
